  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the deployment challenges of large language models (LLMs) in latency- and cost-sensitive applications through efficient compression methods tailored for domain-specific use cases.

Method: The study investigates iterative structural pruning with logit-based and LoRA-based distillation methods, coupled with a blended chain-of-thought supervision loss. Empirical scaling laws are derived by assessing performance trade-offs across various datasets, compression ratios, and supervision formats.

Result: The study reveals predictable degradation in domain-specific task quality under compression, while general-knowledge benchmarks collapse earlier. Chain-of-thought supervision mitigates general knowledge loss caused by pruning. The authors release a domain-specific dataset (FinHeadlineMix) and guidelines.

Conclusion: Supervision formats and chain-of-thought methods are crucial for preserving knowledge in compressed LLMs. The findings provide a framework for making informed compression decisions tailored to specific applications.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across a growing range of domains, yet their scale poses deployment challenges in applications where latency and cost constraints are critical. This paper derives empirical scaling laws for domain-specific LLM compression, quantifying how in-domain and general knowledge performance scale with dataset size, compression ratio, supervision format, and iterative pruning schedule. Using quantitative finance as our application domain, we compare logit-based and LoRA-based distillation under iterative structural pruning, introducing a blended chain-of-thought supervision loss that stabilizes KL-divergence distillation over reasoning traces. In-domain task quality degrades predictably under compression while general-knowledge benchmarks collapse well before the same point; supervision format is the key driver of this tradeoff, with chain-of-thought supervision actively recovering general knowledge that pruning erases. We release the headline dataset FinHeadlineMix, scaling law results, and practical recommendations to provide a reusable framework for domain-specific compression decisions.

</details>


### [61] [Can Scale Save Us From Plasticity Loss in Large Language Models?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24752)
*J. Fernando Hernandez-Garcia,Tomás Figliolia,Beren Millidge*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Plasticity loss, the inability of neural networks to learn new information after learning older tasks, persists in modern GPT-style transformer models and scales sublinearly with model size during multilingual training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether modern large-scale Transformer models face the same challenges of plasticity loss observed in older architectures, especially in natural-language and multilingual domains.

Method: The researchers conducted multilingual continual learning experiments on GPT-style Transformer models (ranging from 5M to 314M parameters) and measured plasticity loss via a probing task in Vietnamese across varying training conditions.

Result: Plasticity loss was evident across model sizes, followed a predictable scaling law that grows sublinearly with model size, and was observed even in stationary multilingual training settings.

Conclusion: Large Transformer models delay but do not eliminate plasticity loss, indicating that continual learning and adaptation issues persist regardless of abrupt task changes or model size increases.

Abstract: The loss of plasticity - the ability of a network to learn new information after having already learned older information - is a fundamental challenge in creating artificial neural networks capable of continual learning. Although this phenomenon has been known for decades, it has mostly been studied in older, relatively small architectures and rarely in natural-language domains. To determine whether loss of plasticity remains a problem in the modern transformer-based LLM paradigm, we study plasticity loss in GPT-style Transformer models trained on a multilingual continual learning problem. Consistent with prior work, we find evidence of plasticity loss across models ranging from 5M to 314M non-embedding parameters, as measured by deterioration on a held-out Vietnamese probing task. We further find that the onset of plasticity loss follows a predictable scaling law, growing sublinearly with model size. These results suggest that larger models may delay the measurable effects of plasticity loss, but that increasing parameter count alone is likely to be insufficient to completely prevent it. We also find evidence of plasticity loss under stationary multilingual training, challenging the view that the phenomenon is exclusive to continual learning with abrupt task changes. Overall, our results suggest that even large Transformer language models trained on natural-language will eventually lose the ability to efficiently adapt to new data after sufficiently long training, in both continual and stationary settings.

</details>


### [62] [BluTrain: A C++/CUDA Framework for AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24780)
*Adhitya Charan,Adwaid Suresh,Anuj Kumar,Aparna A,Dhanakumar K,Dharun M S,Dinesh G,Goutham Kumar Reddy K,Harshini V M,Jenifa D,Jona Delcy C A,Kathirvel S,Killi Uma Maheswara Rao,Kiruthik Kanna M,Kurra Vishnu Sai,Madhumithaa G K,Navin Kumar,Ram Charan Golla,Revathi T,Rishikkanth R,Sanjay Krishna M,Surendra Vendra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BluTrain is a new C++ and CUDA-based deep learning training framework designed to optimize throughput, memory use, and numerical fidelity in training large-scale models. It performs better than existing tools like PyTorch in key metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning model behavior during training relies more on hardware expression than architecture, necessitating a framework that abstracts systems complexity yet offers high hardware control.

Method: The authors designed BluTrain from scratch using C++ and CUDA. The framework includes custom modules for tensors, linear algebra, memory allocation, distributed execution, and compilation.

Result: BluTrain delivered faster token throughput (407K vs. 395K in PyTorch) and used less memory (up to 22% less) in training a GPT-2 model, while maintaining numerical fidelity and achieving slightly better model accuracy.

Conclusion: BluTrain offers an efficient and flexible alternative to existing deep learning frameworks, with potential for further performance tuning and scaling due to its open and native architecture.

Abstract: Progress in deep learning is, at scale, more a matter of systems engineering than of modelling: the behaviour of a model in training (its throughput, its memory footprint, and the numerical fidelity of the result) is determined less by the architecture itself than by how that architecture is expressed on the hardware. To achieve absolute control over this hardware expression while abstracting away systems complexity to make modelling seamless and eliminating the need for repetitive orchestration logic, BluTrain was architected from first principles as a robust, lightweight, and architecture-general training framework in standard C++ and the core CUDA programming model. Every layer is implemented natively: a typed tensor module with reverse-mode autograd, a linear-algebra library, a caching allocator, a multi-mode distributed-execution module, and an MLIR-based deep-learning compiler. In formal evaluations training a 124M-parameter GPT-2 baseline in FP32 on an 8-GPU 6000 Ada system, BluTrain outperforms industry-standard baselines in both throughput (sustaining an average of 407K tokens/s versus PyTorch's 395K tokens/s) and memory efficiency (achieving up to a 22% footprint reduction), while strictly preserving numerical fidelity and converging to a marginally lower final validation loss. With every layer explicitly open to native tuning, the performance ceiling is the framework's own to raise.

</details>


### [63] [Assessing Distribution Shift in Human Activity Recognition for Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24781)
*Rebecca Adaimi,Edison Thomaz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper evaluates the effects of distribution shifts (device type, sensor placement, sampling rate, and user behavior) in Human Activity Recognition (HAR) models and introduces benchmarks for domain generalization in HAR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in understanding the impact of data diversity and distribution shifts on HAR models, especially in real-world settings.

Method: The authors systematically evaluate four types of distribution shifts, introduce benchmarks, and perform a comprehensive evaluation of 28 domain generalization methods in HAR.

Result: The study reveals that diversity shifts uniquely characterize all types of distribution shifts and highlights the limitations of current domain generalization methods which only marginally outperform baseline methods.

Conclusion: This work provides the first systematic investigation into domain generalization for HAR, offering benchmarks and insights to advance future research.

Abstract: While the field of Human Activity Recognition (HAR) continues to draw interest from researchers and advance in important ways, some key challenges remain. One of the most difficult aspects of building HAR models that show good performance in real-world settings is dealing with data diversity from device and sensor heterogeneity, and contextual changes that are intrinsic to real-world applications. While data diversity in HAR has been well-acknowledged in the literature, there remains a gap in understanding the effect of various types of distribution shifts on HAR models and the domain generalization problem that arises. Towards that end, this paper systematically evaluates 4 different types of distribution shifts, including variations in device type, sensor placement, sampling rate, and user behavior. Quantifying their effects, we illustrate that diversity shifts predominantly define all types of shifts, indicating the existence of unique features that are not shared across different domains. We then introduce a uniform HAR-based distribution shift benchmarks and conduct a comprehensive evaluation of up to 28 domain generalization methods. Our analysis exposes the limitations of current domain generalization algorithms in achieving model generalizability, marginally outperforming the empirical risk minimization baseline. This work represents the first systematic exploration of domain generalization and adaptation concerning specific distribution shifts in sensor-based HAR, offering an open-source benchmark platform and datasets to spur further research.

</details>


### [64] [Solving Inverse Problems of Chaotic Systems with Bidirectional Conditional Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24824)
*Peiyan Hu,Jian Zhang,Jiashu Pan,Ruiqi Feng,Tao Zhang,Zhi-Ming Ma,Yuan-Sen Ting,Gongjie Li,Tailin Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Bidirectional Conditional Flow Matching (Bi-CFM) and its variant, Conservation-constrained Bi-CFM (CBi-CFM), to tackle inverse problems in chaotic systems, achieving improved metrics, faster computation, and conservation law respect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inverse problems in chaotic dynamics, specifically inferring initial conditions from final states, are challenging due to ill-posedness, instability, and chaotic time-reverse dynamics.

Method: The authors propose Bi-CFM for bidirectional mappings between initial and final state distributions and CBi-CFM to enforce constraints in systems with conservation laws.

Result: Bi-CFM outperforms baselines in multiple metrics and computation speed. CBi-CFM achieves conservation consistency in the three-body problem and handles real-world globular cluster observations over 10 Gyr evolution.

Conclusion: The proposed methods provide scalable and accurate approaches to solving inverse problems in chaotic systems, advancing modeling in both synthetic and real-world dynamical systems.

Abstract: Modeling chaotic systems is crucial yet challenging. Inverse problems in chaotic dynamics, namely inferring initial conditions from final states, remain largely unsolved because of ill-posedness, non-uniqueness, instability, and potentially chaotic time-reverse dynamics. We address this open problem with Bidirectional Conditional Flow Matching (Bi-CFM), which learns bidirectional mappings between distributions of initial and final states to capture the stochasticity of chaotic evolution and mitigate exponential error accumulation over time. Furthermore, for systems with conservation laws, we extend it to Conservation-constrained Bi-CFM (CBi-CFM). Across the classic Lorenz, Circuit, and high-dimensional Lorenz 96 systems, Bi-CFM improves five distribution-level metrics over baselines while achieving a speedup of more than two orders of magnitude. In the three-body planet-planet scattering problem in planetary dynamics, CBi-CFM better respects conservation laws, with conservation errors comparable to those of the ground truth. Finally, on real observations of globular clusters, collisional million-body systems shaped by $\sim 10^{10}$ years (10 Gyr) of evolution, our method represents an advance in accuracy, establishing a scalable route to solving inverse problems of long-timescale real-world chaotic dynamics.

</details>


### [65] [Difference-Making without Making a Difference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24832)
*Sander Beckers*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Over seven papers, Andreas & Günther presented seven definitions of actual causation under three account types. The latest definition unites these types, revealing their lack of meaningful distinction and compromising all seven proposals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to critically assess Andreas & Günther's attempts to define actual causation and to determine whether their categorization into three account types holds up under scrutiny.

Method: The author analyzes and compares Andreas & Günther's seven definitions of actual causation using crucial examples, focusing on their logical coherence and distinctiveness.

Result: The analysis demonstrates that Andreas & Günther's latest definition conflates the three account types they identified, showing these distinctions to be unfounded.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that all seven definitions and the distinctions between the identified account types by Andreas & Günther are undermined, calling into question the validity of their framework for actual causation.

Abstract: Over a series of seven papers, Andreas & Günther have introduced seven definitions of actual causation and have classified them as belonging to three different, competing, types of accounts: factual difference-making, counterfactual difference-making, and regularity-based. I show that their most recent - factual difference-making - definition instantiates all three types, thereby proving that these are distinctions without a difference. I further compare their novel account to the other six accounts on several crucial examples, revealing that this undermines all seven of their accounts.

</details>


### [66] [Accuracy and Satisfaction in Multi-Turn LLM Dialogues for NFR Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24834)
*Ali Pourghasemi Fatideh,Wilder Baldwin,Maria Dhakal,Collin McMillan,Sepideh Ghanavati*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper evaluates LLM-based dialogues' effectiveness in assessing NFRs related to HIPAA compliance, revealing low accuracy despite user agreement and providing design insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of evaluation benchmarks for LLM-based dialogue assistants in handling Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs), which are ambiguous, context-dependent, and require collaborative reasoning.

Method: 49 programmers used GitHub Copilot to evaluate 148 HIPAA-derived NFRs against the iTrust codebase over three dimensions: requirement satisfaction, reasoning, and code localization.

Result: Developers often aligned with LLM outputs, but comparisons with expert ground truth showed low accuracy. User satisfaction decreased with lengthy responses but improved with proactive interactions.

Conclusion: The study highlights the need for improvements in LLM dialogue systems for NFR assessment and provides insights into user satisfaction factors and interaction design.

Abstract: LLM-based dialogue assistants have become mainstream tools for software developers, yet current evaluation benchmarks focus exclusively on functional correctness. This leaves a critical gap in assessing the quality and accuracy of these conversations when handling Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs), which are inherently vague, context-dependent, and involve many parts of a program. Evaluating how well these systems support collaborative reasoning about NFRs requires methods that go beyond single-turn accuracy to capture both the correctness of the system's outputs and the quality of the multi-turn interaction. In this paper, we investigate the accuracy and quality of multi-turn conversations between developers and an LLM-based agent in the domain of Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) regulatory compliance. We hired 49 programmers to interact with GitHub Copilot to assess 148 HIPAA-derived NFRs against the iTrust codebase, a system designed to comply with HIPAA regulations, across three dimensions: requirement satisfaction level, reasoning, and code localization. We find that developers tend to agree with LLM assessments, but accuracy against expert ground truth is low. We model user satisfaction and find that longer system responses and more information-providing turns negatively affect user satisfaction, whereas proactive interactions positively affect it. Our findings provide insights for designing LLM-based dialogue systems that support NFR assessment.

</details>


### [67] [Grading the Grader: Lessons from Evaluating an Agentic Data Analysis System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24839)
*Tian Zheng,Kai-Tai Hsu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper evaluates agentic data-analysis systems and their complex outputs by pairing human review with automated grading strategies to achieve high precision and recall rates, focusing on enhancing grading reliability and advancing grading pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome evaluation challenges posed by rich outputs generated by agentic systems and to differentiate genuine output discrepancies from grading errors in multi-turn tasks.

Method: The researchers developed a three-layer grading system combining strict regex matching, LLM-based lenient grading, and human inspection as part of a grading cascade for dataset tasks. Advances include keyword-anchored pipelines and iterative nudging mechanisms.

Result: Strict and lenient automated graders achieved 100% observed precision. Recall rates were optimized, with iterative nudging vastly improving grading success and lenient-pass rates. Variable type (task metadata) showed strong associations with grading outcomes.

Conclusion: The multi-layer grading pipeline effectively resolves grading challenges, combining AI-based methods and human oversight for accurate evaluation of data-analysis systems. Iterative nudging enhances success, and different graders complement each other's strengths.

Abstract: Agentic data analysis systems produce rich outputs, including code, numerical results, and verbal diagnostics. This makes them more challenging to evaluate than single-turn LLM responses. It is therefore necessary to distinguish genuine disagreement between an agent's output and a ground-truth answer from grading artifacts. We investigate how reliably automated graders assess such a system and what strategies improve grading quality by applying LAMBDA, a multi-agent data-analysis system, on 153 numerical QRData tasks from DSGym. We develop and evaluate a three-layer human-AI grading cascade: strict regex matching, LLM-based lenient grading, and snippet-based human inspection, which combines non-GenAI and GenAI strategies with different failure profiles. Both automated graders achieve 100% observed precision (0/70 false positives). The lenient grader's recall is 97% against human labels. A keyword-anchored extraction pipeline raises the strict grader's recall by 60 percentage points over a last-number heuristic; the lenient grader is architecturally parser-independent. An iterative nudge mechanism raises grading run success from 36% to 97% and lenient-pass rates from 16% to 46%; comparing nudging with and without original-question re-injection shows that re-injection offers no benefit, confirming the nudge as an answer template cue. We further observe in this case study that variable type is the task metadata field most consistently associated with grading pipeline dynamics and observed outcome grades.

</details>


### [68] [Matching Tasks to Objectives: Fine-Tuning and Prompt-Tuning Strategies for Encoder-Decoder Pre-trained Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24841)
*Ahmad Pouramini,Hesham Faili*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores the impact of diverse pre-training objectives on encoder-decoder language models and introduces the Match Task to Objective (MTO) framework to enhance task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the need for improving task-specific performance of encoder-decoder pre-trained language models by aligning pre-training and fine-tuning objectives with diverse tasks.

Method: The authors introduce the MTO framework, which automates the selection of appropriate objectives for tasks, prepares task-related data for adaptation via unsupervised training, and designs templates to align objectives across training and fine-tuning stages.

Result: The proposed strategies achieve over 120% performance gains in few-shot scenarios and outperform baselines in full-dataset settings. They also enhance performance for prompt-tuning methodologies.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the significance of aligning pre-training, adaptation, and fine-tuning objectives for specific tasks, presenting a versatile framework that improves performance across various scenarios.

Abstract: Prompt-based learning has emerged as a dominant paradigm in natural language processing. This study explores the impact of diverse pre-training objectives on the performance of encoder-decoder pre-trained language models across generation and question answering tasks, with a focus on commonsense knowledge retrieval and completion. We highlight the benefits of incorporating multiple objectives during both pre-training and fine-tuning stages. We introduce the Match Task to Objective (MTO) framework and methods for determining the appropriate objective for a given task. This framework offers automated methods to prepare task-related data for adaptation through unsupervised training, based on the identified objective. In the fine-tuning stage, we design novel templates that align with the objectives of the pre-training and adaptation stages. When aligned with task requirements, these strategies can achieve a performance gain of over 120\% compared to conventional methods in few-shot settings. They significantly outperform related works in few-shot settings and exceed the baseline even in full-dataset scenarios. Furthermore, we extend this approach to include prompt-tuning methodologies, providing guidance for more effective soft prompt engineering and optimization. Our strategies significantly enhance prompt-tuning performance as well. These insights hold substantial value, precisely guiding the selection and optimization of models customized for specific tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/puraminy/MTO/

</details>


### [69] [World Models in Pieces: Structural Certification for General Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24842)
*Yikai Lu,Yifei Wu,Xinyu Lu,Tongxin Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces "structural certification" to evaluate general agents in a specialized world model, providing localized, certifiable guarantees for long-horizon planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitation of standard worst-case analysis for general agents, as it fails to distinguish between critical performance areas and irrelevant failures in a specialized world model.

Method: The authors propose a transition-local framework called structural certification, leveraging deep compositional goals to derive entry-specific performance guarantees. They provide algorithms with an error-bound analysis of $O(1/n) + O(δ)$.

Result: The paper proves that general agents, under deep compositional goals, achieve a structural world model with tight error bounds in certain regimes, enabling certifiable reliability in critical transitions during long-horizon planning.

Conclusion: Structural certification offers a new path to evaluate and certify general agents, particularly in specialized tasks, by focusing on local transitions where performance guarantees can be reliably established.

Abstract: In the big-world regime, agents cannot be universally capable and their ability is inevitably specialized across a world model in pieces. Consequently, standard uniform guarantees fail to distinguish between the understanding of critical bottlenecks and irrelevant failures. We first formalize this limitation by proving that general agents are not universal, rendering standard worst-case analysis uninformative. To overcome this, we introduce structural certification, a transition-local framework that maps bounded goal-conditioned performance to entry-wise guarantees on the agent's internal world model. Our main contribution is constructive. We provide algorithms that filter specific transitions using deep compositional goals and prove that a general agent on these goals has a structural world model with a $\mathcal{O}(1/n) + \mathcal{O}(δ)$ error bound. Conversely, this bound is tight in the small-$δ$ regime, whose existence is explicitly guaranteed by our certification. These results enable the certifiable deployment of general agents by localizing the specific transitions where long-horizon planning is reliable.

</details>


### [70] [OpenThoughts-Agent: Data Recipes for Agentic Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24855)
*Negin Raoof,Richard Zhuang,Marianna Nezhurina,Etash Guha,Atula Tejaswi,Ryan Marten,Charlie F. Ruan,Tyler Griggs,Alexander Glenn Shaw,Hritik Bansal,E. Kelly Buchanan,Artem Gazizov,Reinhard Heckel,Chinmay Hegde,Sankalp Jajee,Daanish Khazi,Emmanouil Koukoumidis,Xiangyi Li,Hange Liu,Shlok Natarajan,Harsh Raj,Nicholas Roberts,Ethan Shen,Nishad Singhi,Michael Siu,Ashima Suvarna,Hanwen Xing,Patrick Yubeaton,Robert Zhang,Leon Liangyu Chen,Xiaokun Chen,Steven Dillmann,Saadia Gabriel,Xunyi Jiang,Anurag Kashyap,Boxuan Li,Yein Park,Minh Pham,Sujay Sanghavi,Lin Shi,Ke Sun,Yixin Wang,Zhiwei Xu,Erica Zhang,Siyan Zhao,Wanjia Zhao,Jenia Jitsev,Alex Dimakis,Benjamin Feuer,Ludwig Schmidt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces the OpenThoughts-Agent project, focusing on creating an open data curation pipeline for training broadly capable agentic language models, achieving significant performance improvements over existing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is limited public knowledge about how to curate training data for broadly capable agentic language models, and current efforts lack generalizability across diverse tasks.

Method: The authors developed a fully open data curation pipeline and conducted over 100 ablation experiments to refine training methods. They created a dataset of 100K examples and fine-tuned the Qwen3-32B model.

Result: Fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on data from the pipeline achieved 44.8% average accuracy on seven benchmarks, a 3.9% improvement over the best existing model. The training data demonstrated strong scaling properties.

Conclusion: The OpenThoughts-Agent project provides valuable resources and insights for advancing research on broadly capable agentic models, and all components are publicly released to foster open research.

Abstract: Agentic language models dramatically expand the applications of AI yet little is publicly known about how to curate training data for broadly capable agents. Existing open efforts such as SWE-Smith, SERA, and Nemotron-Terminal typically target a single benchmark, leaving open the question of how to train models that generalize across diverse agentic tasks. The OpenThoughts-Agent (OT-Agent) project addresses this gap with a fully open data curation pipeline for training agentic models. We conduct more than 100 controlled ablation experiments to systematically investigate each stage of the pipeline, yielding insights on the importance of task sources and diversity. We then assemble a training set of 100K examples from our pipeline and fine-tune Qwen3-32B on this dataset, which yields an average accuracy of 44.8% across seven agentic benchmarks and a 3.9 percentage point improvement over the strongest existing open data agentic model (Nemotron-Terminal-32B, 40.9%). Moreover, our training data exhibits strong scaling properties, outperforming alternative open datasets at every training set size in compute-controlled comparisons. We publicly release our training sets, data pipeline, experimental data, and models at openthoughts.ai to support future open research on agentic model training.

</details>


<div id='cs.AR'></div>

# cs.AR [[Back]](#toc)

### [71] [PCB-QA: Evaluating LLMs over the First Printed Circuit Board Design Question-Answer Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23704)
*Sahana Srinivasan,Benjamin Tan,Benjamin Turnbull,Hammond Pearce*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces PCB-QA, a dataset of 480 question-answer pairs for assessing the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) in understanding PCB design files. Benchmarking results indicate that LLMs perform better using textual formats.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the success of LLMs in IC design, their use in PCB design is underexplored due to the lack of text-based PCB datasets and effective methods for prompting LLMs with PCB files.

Method: The authors created a dataset (PCB-QA) from 8 open-source hardware projects and experimented with LLMs using different PCB file representations, including graphical PDFs, native design formats, and text-based formats.

Result: The benchmarking of 4 state-of-the-art LLMs revealed that Gemini 3 Flash Preview achieves 93% accuracy using a JSON-based textual format, demonstrating the potential for evaluating PCB designs using LLMs.

Conclusion: Textual representations of PCB design files are effective for LLM evaluation, and the PCB-QA dataset provides a foundation for integrating LLMs into the PCB design lifecycle.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated capabilities in electronic design automation (EDA) for integrated circuits. However, their applications in printed circuit board (PCB) design and analysis tasks remain underexplored. In part, this is due to a (1) a lack of text-based PCB datasets to evaluate LLMs and (2) a lack of methodologies for prompting LLMs with different types of PCB design files. To address this gap, our paper proposes PCB-QA: a manually created questionnaire dataset amounting to 480 question-answer pairs for PCBs, derived from 8 different open-source hardware projects of varying complexities. We examine multiple aspects of PCB designs and cover questions about component connections, datasheet examination, and simulation data obtainable via SPICE. Using our dataset as a benchmark, we prompt LLMs with different representations (and combinations) of PCB design files and record observations. This allows us to measure, for the first time, if LLMs can understand schematics and netlists in their "native forms" (i.e. graphical PDFs, KiCAD-format design files) or if textual formats are preferred. Including both commercial and open-weight models, we benchmark 4 state-of-the-art LLMs on our dataset, finding that Gemini 3 Flash Preview can answer questions with an accuracy of 93% using a proposed JSON-based textual format. This demonstrates that text-based PCB design formats can be evaluated by LLMs. Our open-source questionnaire is the first step towards enabling LLM integrations within the PCB design life cycle.

</details>


### [72] [VeriPilot: An LLM-Powered Verilog Debugging Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23759)
*Yihan Wang,Cheng Liu,Jiazheng Zhang,Lei Zhang,Long Cheng,Xiaowei Li,Huawei Li*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: VeriPilot is a debugging framework for Verilog that uses LLMs, golden reference models, and static analysis to improve debugging efficiency and repair success in complex designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Debugging Verilog code is time-consuming and challenging, especially for long dependency chains and large codebases, necessitating a more effective solution.

Method: VeriPilot uses LLMs for internal variable alignment with golden models, combined with Control-Data-Flow Graphs for signal tracing and minimal code region identification for effective bug localization and repair.

Result: Experiments on NVIDIA’s CVDP benchmark showed VeriPilot enhances GPT-4's debugging success rate from 54.3% to 85.71%, improving overall efficiency.

Conclusion: VeriPilot significantly outperforms standard debugging approaches for Verilog designs, making it a robust solution for automated localization and repair.

Abstract: Verilog debugging remains one of the most time-consuming stages in digital circuit design. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled automated debugging; however, most existing approaches rely solely on test outputs and compiler feedback in an end-to-end manner, limiting their effectiveness on complex bugs. A key challenge is that the root cause of an error may be far removed from its observable outputs, making it difficult for LLMs to trace long dependency chains in code. This challenge is further exacerbated in large codebases, where long context lengths hinder efficient reasoning. To address these limitations, we propose VeriPilot, an LLM-powered debugging framework that leverages golden reference models to enable fine-grained bug localization and repair. VeriPilot goes beyond output-level comparison by aligning internal variable semantics between the Verilog design and its corresponding golden model through LLM-based analysis. It then performs step-by-step signal tracing using Control-Data-Flow Graphs (CDFGs) derived from static analysis, identifying a minimal set of suspicious code regions along with their correct counterparts from the golden model. These structured insights are subsequently provided to the LLM to guide reasoning and automated code repair. Experimental results on the Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems (CVDP) benchmark from NVIDIA demonstrate that VeriPilot improves the repair success rate of GPT-4o from 54.3\% to 85.71\%, significantly enhancing both bug localization accuracy and repair effectiveness for complex Verilog designs. The source code and benchmark are publicly available at Github https://github.com/YihanWn/VeriPilot.git.

</details>


<div id='cs.CL'></div>

# cs.CL [[Back]](#toc)

### [73] [EXPO-SQL: Execution-based Clause-level Policy Optimization for Text-to-SQL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23693)
*Jaehoon Lee,CheolWon Na,Suyoung Bae,Jin-Seop Lee,Jihyung Lee,YunSeok Choi,Jee-Hyong Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EXPO-SQL enhances Text-to-SQL tasks by providing fine-grained clause-level rewards instead of uniform query-level rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL methods in Text-to-SQL treat correct and incorrect SQL clauses equally, leading to insufficient training signals.

Method: It uses clause-level rewards based on execution results, analyzing error messages and incremental query evaluations.

Result: Experiments show EXPO-SQL outperforms other supervised and RL-based approaches in Text-to-SQL benchmarks.

Conclusion: Fine-grained clause-level learning boosts the generation of accurate executable SQL queries in Text-to-SQL tasks.

Abstract: Text-to-SQL enables users to query databases using natural language by generating executable SQL queries. Recent methods have increasingly adopted Large Language Models based reinforcement learning (RL) to leverage execution feedback for training. However, existing RL methods assign uniform query-level rewards to all clauses in a SQL query, treating correct and incorrect clauses equally. This coarse-grained reward design leads to insufficient learning signals for correct SQL generation. To address this issue, we propose EXPO-SQL (EXecution-based clause-level Policy Optimization for Text-to-SQL) which provides fine-grained supervision through clause-level rewards. To assign clause-level rewards, our method identifies erroneous clauses by analyzing execution results, including error messages and clause-wise incremental execution. Experiments on widely-used Text-to-SQL benchmarks demonstrate that EXPO-SQL significantly outperforms existing supervised fine-tuning, prompting, and RL-based methods through fine-grained clause-level learning. Our code is available at https://github. com/jhn25/EXPO-SQL.

</details>


### [74] [ModTGCN: Modularity-aware Graph Neural Networks for Text Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23694)
*Rajarshi Misra,Aditya Sharma,Vinti Agarwal,Hari Om Aggrawal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces ModTGCN, a graph neural network that improves text classification by incorporating modularity to enhance class-consistent document clustering and improve scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-based text classification models often ignore global community structures in semantic document graphs, which can blur class boundaries and lead to model inefficiencies.

Method: The method introduces ModTGCN, which optimizes both cross-entropy and a modularity-based auxiliary objective. It also decouples the heterogeneous TextGCN graph into separate components, improving scalability and introducing label-aware reweighting and modularity strategy options.

Result: Experiments across five benchmarks demonstrated consistent performance improvements, particularly in complex datasets with low homophily, such as Ohsumed and 20NG.

Conclusion: ModTGCN effectively enhances the classification accuracy of text graphs by combining modularity-based auxiliary objectives with scalable graph construction, showcasing its utility in complex text classification tasks.

Abstract: Graph-based text classification models typically rely on local neighborhood aggregation and overlook global community structure, despite semantic document graphs exhibiting strong class-consistent clustering. Ignoring this can blur class boundaries and lead to over-smoothing. We propose ModTGCN, a modularity-aware graph neural network for text classification that jointly optimizes cross-entropy and a modularity-based auxiliary objective to promote class-coherent document communities while preserving discriminative representations. The modularity term is computed on a document-document similarity graph derived from transformer embeddings (pretrained or fine-tuned). To improve scalability, we decouple the original heterogeneous TextGCN graph into separate document-word and word-word components, achieving 2x-10x faster training. We further study graph construction strategies, label-aware edge reweighting, and supervision choices for modularity optimization. Experiments on five benchmarks show consistent gains, with larger improvements on complex, low homophily datasets such as Ohsumed and 20NG.

</details>


### [75] [Quantifying Prior Dominance in RAG Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23695)
*Barak Or*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a metric, Normalized Context Utilization (NCU), to evaluate grounding methods in language models, finding small models outperform larger ones in strict factual tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenge the reliance on traditional scaling laws of large language models (LLMs) by analyzing their capacity for genuine contextual information extraction.

Method: Develops NCU metric to assess context grounding, tested on models ranging from 1.5B to 72B parameters and a commercial API under zero-shot, oracle, and adversarial conditions.

Result: Small Language Models (SLMs) achieve comparable or superior results to larger architectures for strict factual extraction, while commercial APIs suffer from parametric memory overriding external evidence.

Conclusion: SLMs have structural advantages in strict extraction workflows, questioning the necessity of scaling for certain applications

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds Large Language Models in external knowledge, yet current evaluations rely on discrete heuristics that suffer from ''epistemic blindness'' - failing to distinguish genuine contextual information extraction from parametric memory recall. To address this, we introduce the Normalized Context Utilization (NCU) metric, leveraging continuous token log-probabilities across zero-shot, oracle, and adversarial conditions to strictly quantify contextual information gain. Evaluating architectures ranging from 1.5B to 72B parameters alongside a proprietary commercial API reveals that for strict factual extraction (without Chain-of-Thought reasoning), traditional scaling laws exhibit extreme diminishing returns: highly efficient Small Language Models (SLMs) match or outperform high-capacity architectures. Furthermore, we demonstrate that ``Prior Dominance'' correlates with model scale and proprietary alignments. The evaluated commercial API not only overrode explicit external evidence in nearly half of adversarial conflicts, but also frequently suffered from systemic confidence collapse (Negative Transfer) when its parametric priors were contradicted. Our findings highlight the structural epistemic advantage and superior contextual adherence of SLMs in strict extraction workflows.

</details>


### [76] [Self-Recognition Finetuning can Prevent and Reverse Emergent Misalignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23700)
*Arush Tagade,Shaoheng Zhou,Jiaxin Wen,Shi Feng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates "emergent misalignment" (EM) in language models, analyzing how character-targeted interventions can prevent misalignment by stabilizing an aligned persona. It introduces self-generated text recognition (SGTR) finetuning and compares it to traditional methods, showing its effectiveness in both reversing and preventing EM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this study is to understand and mitigate emergent misalignment (EM) in language models, which disrupts their aligned character and leads to unintended negative behavior.

Method: The authors propose self-generated text recognition (SGTR) finetuning, a character-targeted intervention, as a novel approach to mitigate EM. They conduct two-stage finetuning experiments on multiple large language models and datasets to evaluate its performance against traditional finetuning benchmarks.

Result: SGTR finetuning successfully prevents and reverses EM with consistent improvements in aligned persona stabilization compared to traditional methods. It avoids unintended consequences and highlights the linkage of EM with destabilization of aligned character rather than harmful learning.

Conclusion: This paper concludes that EM originates more from character destabilization than coherent misaligned personas, and SGTR finetuning is an effective targeted strategy to reinforce aligned behaviors in language models.

Abstract: Emergent misalignment (EM) has been linked to the activation of misaligned persona vectors and evil character traits, suggesting that EM operates through disruption of the model's aligned character rather than direct learning of harmful content. Motivated by this connection, we study self-generated text recognition (SGTR) finetuning as a character-targeted intervention that is distinct from existing in-training defenses. We conduct two-stage finetuning experiments across three models (GPT-4.1, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, Seed-OSS-36B-Instruct) and multiple EM datasets to compare SGTR finetuning against benign finetuning baselines (correct domain-specific data, general knowledge, and word counting) to find it an effective defense in both reversal and prevention settings. We find that all interventions produce comparable EM reversal, but only when restoring capabilities that EM had degraded. For prevention, only SGTR finetuning consistently reduces misalignment without exacerbating any individual metric, suggesting that character fortification specifically drives prevention. We provide further evidence for EM's relation to the LLM's default character by showing that EM finetuning induces diversity into the LLM's identity self-reports, artificially corrupting self-recognition exacerbates misalignment caused by EM finetuning, and that removing the model's identity-bearing system prompt substantially reduces the effect of EM finetuning. Together, these findings reframe EM not as the adoption of a coherent misaligned persona but as the destabilization of aligned character.

</details>


### [77] [Evaluating LLM Usage for Efficient and Explainable Numerical and Classified Implicit Sentiment Analysis of Product Desirability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23701)
*Sherri Weitl-Harms,John Hastings*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a scalable framework using LLMs to assess product sentiment from qualitative feedback. Achieving high accuracy and correlation compared to human annotations, it supports practical uses like product development and marketing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in measuring nuanced user sentiment from qualitative product feedback, especially without explicit review scores, and support scalable, interpretable evaluation.

Method: The authors evaluated LLMs using two Product Desirability Toolkit (PDT) datasets with zero-shot sentiment scoring and classification, comparing performances with human annotations and baseline approaches.

Result: LLMs demonstrated high correlation (up to 0.97) and accuracy (up to 94%) with expert labels, outperforming lexicon-based and transformer baselines. GPT-4o-mini provided comparable results at significantly lower cost.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively captures sentiment and improves interpretability and transparency through confidence ratings and rationale explanations, promoting its practical use for product evaluation and development.

Abstract: Qualitative product feedback can reveal nuanced user experiences, but its implicit sentiment is difficult to measure. This paper presents a scalable and interpretable framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to quantify product desirability from such data. Using two Product Desirability Toolkit (PDT) datasets from ZORQ and CARMA comprising 106 respondent term groupings with gold-standard human annotation, zero-shot continuous numerical sentiment scoring and categorical sentiment classification are evaluated without relying on explicit review scores. Across the datasets, LLMs generated numerical sentiment scores directly from qualitative responses and closely matched expert labels, achieving Pearson correlations up to 0.97 and classification accuracy up to 94%. LLMs maintained robustness even when handling data presented in multiple forms and consistently expressed high confidence. In contrast, lexicon-based and transformer baselines did not produce statistically significant results. Among the models tested, GPT-4o-mini achieved performance comparable to larger models at 94% lower cost, supporting scalable deployment.
  The framework also incorporates model confidence ratings and human-readable rationale explanations (xAI), improving interpretability, transparency, and trust while supporting practical use in product satisfaction assessment. In general, using the PDT tool as a survey method along with a cost efficient LLM for sentiment analysis has the potential to provide for product evaluation with results that are rich in terms of sentiment scores (both numerical and classified sentiment) and in terms of the high-level user impressions of the product that can be used to identify ideas for product development and improvement, as well as marketing ideas for target audiences.

</details>


### [78] [Ground Then Rank: Revisiting Knowledge-Based VQA with Training-Free Entity Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23881)
*Qian Ma,Qiong Wu,Zhengyi Zhou,Yao Ma*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper addresses challenges in knowledge-based visual question answering (KB-VQA) by introducing a training-free method using a two-step process for better entity and fact grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-modal retrieval augmented generation (MM-RAG) methods struggle with integrating fine-grained entity and evidence-level groundings due to tightly coupled processes, resulting in high cost and limited generalization.

Method: The proposed framework, Identify-Before-Answer (IBA), decouples entity identification and section-level evidence re-ranking. It uses a multi-modal large language model (MLLM) to identify high-confidence entities from candidate names and a textual re-ranker for evidence selection.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that IBA outperforms fine-tuned multi-modal re-ranking baselines on Encyclopedic-VQA and InfoSeek tasks, achieving better accuracy while reducing training and inference complexity.

Conclusion: IBA framework simplifies KB-VQA by improving both entity identification and evidence selection processes, offering a superior and efficient alternative to existing methods.

Abstract: Knowledge-Based Visual Question Answering (KB-VQA) requires grounding visual queries to external knowledge beyond directly observable content in images. While recent multi modal large language models (MLLMs) show strong perceptual abilities, they struggle on KB-VQA tasks requiring groundings from both fine-grained entity and evidence levels. Most existing multi-modal retrieval augmented generation (MM-RAG) methods tightly couple entity discrimination and section-level evidence ranking into a single re-ranking stage, leading to high cost and limited generalization. In this work, we revisit existing MM-RAG solutions from a workflow perspective and argue both entity-level and fact-level groundings are key bottlenecks. We observe that although MLLMs often fail under open-ended entity naming, they can better identify the correct entity when selecting from a small set of candidate names. Based on this insight, we propose a simple and training-free identify-before-answer IBA framework that decouples entity identification from section-level re-ranking. Our approach prompts an MLLM to select high-confidence entities using only candidate names, followed by an off-the-shelf textual re-ranker for evidence selection. Experiments on Encyclopedic-VQA and InfoSeek show that our method consistently outperforms fine-tuned multi-modal re-ranking baselines while reducing training and inference complexity. Additional analyses reveal that the improvements arise not only from better entity identification, but also from selecting more informative evidence once correct entity is fixed. Our implementation is made public to ease reproducibility.

</details>


### [79] [One Year Later...The Harms Persist, But So Do We!](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23884)
*Annika Marie Schoene,Cansu Canca,Gautham Vijay Kumar,Anson Antony*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates six LLMs for safety in mental health-related conversations, revealing high failure rates in several conditions despite reliable safeguards in suicide/self-harm scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Increasing use of LLMs in mental health conversations with inadequate safeguards across various clinical conditions.

Method: Evaluation of six LLMs across 16 DSM-5 conditions using four adversarial attack variants, an eight-dimension harm taxonomy, and a multi-dimensional evaluation framework.

Result: Safeguards perform reliably only for suicide and self-harm, with failure rates reaching up to 100% for other conditions like eating disorders and substance use disorder.

Conclusion: Ethical design requires well-defined harm categories and proper safeguards; without them, LLMs pose significant risks to vulnerable groups, especially in education.

Abstract: General-purpose large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for mental health-related conversations, yet safety safeguards remain inadequate and inconsistent across clinical conditions. This study evaluates six proprietary LLMs across 16 DSM-5 conditions using four adversarial attack variants, introducing an eight-dimension harm taxonomy and a multi-dimensional evaluation framework. Results show that safeguards hold reliably only for suicide and self-harm, while conditions such as eating disorders, substance use disorder, and major depressive disorder exhibit failure rates of up to 100%. We argue that ethical design and deployment of these LLMs demand clearly defined harm categories across clinical conditions and implementation of safeguards accordingly. Until such safeguards are in place, these models pose significant risks to vulnerable populations, making their growing integration into educational settings a particularly concerning.

</details>


### [80] [AI-PAVE-Br: Leveraging Large Language Models for Enhanced Product Attribute Value Extraction through a Golden Set Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24655)
*Murilo Gazzola,Hugo Gobato Souto,Samuel Silva,Júlia Schubert Peixoto,Felipe Siqueira,André Luis Pedroso de Morais,Caio Gomes*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces AI-PAVEBr, an advanced system for extracting product attribute values in Brazilian e-commerce and provides a new benchmark dataset called the Golden Set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid expansion and complexity of Brazilian e-commerce have created challenges in accurately extracting structured product data, particularly given the linguistic diversity of Portuguese.

Method: The researchers developed AI-PAVEBr, leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) via prompt engineering. Additionally, they curated and shared the Golden Set dataset, which is manually annotated for Product Attribute Value Extraction (PAVE).

Result: AI-PAVEBr significantly outperforms traditional Named Entity Recognition (NER) models in product data extraction tasks.

Conclusion: AI-PAVEBr offers a scalable, high-accuracy solution tailored for Brazilian e-commerce. This research also provides a valuable dataset, advancing the NLP field and encouraging subsequent studies in non-English markets.

Abstract: The explosive growth and complexity of product data within the dynamic Brazilian e-commerce landscape demand robust and specialized methods for structured information extraction. Traditional approaches to Product Attribute Value Extraction (PAVE) often struggle with the linguistic nuances and sheer diversity of product descriptions in Portuguese. To address this critical gap, this paper introduces two major contributions. First, we present AI-PAVEBr, a specialized system engineered with Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform high-accuracy PAVE specifically for Brazilian e-commerce catalogs. Second, to facilitate reproducible research and provide a definitive benchmark, we introduce and share the Golden Set, a new, meticulously curated, and manually annotated dataset for PAVE in Portuguese. We detail the creation process and structure (Entity, Category, Subcategories) of this high-quality reference set. Our experiments conclusively show that AI-PAVE-Br, leveraging targeted prompt engineering, dramatically outperforms conventional Named Entity Recognition (NER) baselines. This work not only delivers a superior, scalable solution for a major non-English market but also enriches the NLP community with a valuable, publicly available resource for future PAVE research.

</details>


### [81] [Do LLM Attribution Metrics Transfer? Auditing Retrieval-Augmented Generation Evaluation Across Datasets and Constructs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23915)
*Tianyu Ding,Aditya Nannapaneni,Juan Pablo De la Cruz Weinstein*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper audits eight automatic scorers for attribution in LLM retrieval-augmented generation under different evaluation areas, finding that no single scorer consistently performs well across all datasets in multi-dataset constructs. Metric choice needs dataset-specific validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore and address the reliability and transferability of automatic metrics for evaluating attribution in LLM retrieval-augmented generation.

Method: Auditing eight scoring methods across varied constructs, with human-labeled datasets, statistical tests, and comparisons to manual prompt-based LLM evaluation.

Result: No automatic scorer consistently transfers across datasets in attribution constructs, demonstrating variability and collapsing in performance for some metrics like NLI. LLM prompt-based judgments avoid some failures but are expensive and inconsistent.

Conclusion: Reliability of automatic scorers for attribution evaluation is dataset-dependent, necessitating validation for every target dataset instead of generalizing from others.

Abstract: Practice often treats automatic metrics for attribution in LLM retrieval-augmented generation as interchangeable. We audit eight automatic scorers -- lexical, embedding, and BERTScore baselines alongside entailment/grounding-trained models (clean and FEVER NLI, the checker MiniCheck) -- across three evaluation constructs (provenance/topicality, generated-answer attribution, and fact-check entailment), asking whether any scorer transfers: stays within the 95% confidence interval of the best audited scorer on every dataset of a multi-dataset construct. In the construct with the most multi-dataset human-labeled coverage -- generated-answer attribution (AttributionBench's four source datasets, n = 1,610, with independent HAGRID, n = 2,150) -- none does: the per-dataset metric rankings invert (Kendall tau = -0.64, p = 0.031 on AttributedQA vs. LFQA), and an off-the-shelf NLI scorer that is best on short-claim AttributedQA (AUROC 0.90) collapses to AUROC 0.53 (chance) on long-form LFQA, where BERTScore wins (0.91); the flip is not a length or truncation artifact. This instability has a concrete decision cost: a naive "best-on-average" rule for choosing an evaluator fails leave-one-dataset-out (mean held-out regret 0.172 AUROC, worse than fixing one scorer), so metric choice must be validated on the target dataset rather than learned from others. A prompt-based LLM judge avoids the chance-level collapses the automatic scorers suffer (no LFQA collapse) but is not uniformly best, ~100x costlier, and non-deterministic -- relocating, not removing, the validation burden.

</details>


### [82] [When Retrieval Metrics Mislead: Measuring Policy Signal in Long-Horizon Tool-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23937)
*Tianyu Ding,Juan Pablo De la Cruz Weinstein*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines the effectiveness of exact-match retrieval recall as a proxy for policy utility in decision models using tau-bench and Qwen2.5 classifiers. It finds that exact-match recall may underestimate downstream utility, as retrieval-based performance is comparable to gold-standard inputs in some configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: This study aims to evaluate whether exact-match retrieval recall is a reliable proxy for the utility of retrieved policies in pre-action classification tasks, addressing gaps in understanding policy-decision dynamics in tau-bench.

Method: The researchers tested classifiers (Qwen2.5-3B/7B) under gold-policy and retrieved-policy conditions, using structured state representations and varying fine-tuning configurations to assess macro-F1 performance differences.

Result: While exact-match retrieval yielded the governing clause as rank 1 for 7% of cases, the performance under retrieved policies was close to that of gold clauses, with macro-F1 differences not statistically significant in this setup.

Conclusion: Exact-match clause recall alone may not sufficiently capture the downstream utility of retrieved policies. Incorporating retrieved policies into the classification process provides a more accurate evaluation framework.

Abstract: Exact-match retrieval recall is often used as a proxy for whether a retriever supplies useful policy context to a downstream decision model. We test this proxy for pre-action policy classification in tau-bench using Qwen2.5-3B/7B classifiers. Under gold-policy conditioning, a compact structured state improves macro-F1 over raw trajectories by 0.13-0.17 after tuning. We then replace the benchmark-designated policy clause with the top-ranked clause retrieved from decision-time context. Although the exact governing clause is retrieved at rank 1 for only 7% of airline states, the primary 3B classifier obtains macro-F1 0.58 with retrieved clauses versus 0.60 with gold clauses (Delta=-0.02, task-cluster 95% CI [-0.23,+0.21]); mismatched-policy and no-policy controls score 0.32 and 0.21. We do not detect a macro-F1 difference between retrieved and gold clauses in this configuration, although the interval remains too wide to establish non-inferiority. The same qualitative pattern appears with a second retriever and at 7B, while varying across fine-tuning configurations. These results indicate that exact-match clause recall can underestimate downstream policy utility in this benchmark setting, motivating evaluation with retrieved policies in the classification loop rather than recall alone.

</details>


### [83] [QuechuaTok: Morphological Boundary Accuracy as a Necessary Metric for Tokenizer Evaluation in Agglutinative Low-Resource Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23943)
*Maria Contreras*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces QuechuaTok, a benchmark for evaluating tokenization strategies for Southern Quechua. It compares four tokenizers, showing that fertility rates are insufficient to measure tokenizers for agglutinative languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard tokenization evaluation metrics fail to capture morphological correctness in agglutinative languages like Southern Quechua.

Method: The paper evaluates four tokenization strategies using metrics like fertility rate, OOV rate, and morphology boundary accuracy, leveraging a 200k-sentence corpus and a finite-state morphological analyzer.

Result: BPE achieved the lowest fertility rate but very low MorphAcc (6.67%), while PRPE reached the highest MorphAcc (83.33%), highlighting inadequacies in relying solely on fertility rate for evaluation.

Conclusion: Morphological correctness must be prioritized over traditional metrics like fertility rate for tokenizing agglutinative languages, as demonstrated by PRPE's superior morphological boundary accuracy.

Abstract: Tokenization is a foundational step in NLP pipelines, yet standard evaluation metrics such as fertility rate fail to capture morphological correctness for agglutinative languages. We present QuechuaTok, a systematic benchmark comparing four tokenization strategies - BPE, Unigram LM, WordPiece, and a morphology-aware PRPE tokenizer - for Southern Quechua (quz), a low-resource agglutinative language spoken by 8-10 million people in South America. Using a 200k-sentence corpus and the SQUOIA finite-state morphological analyzer (Rios, 2016) as silver standard, we evaluate three metrics: fertility rate, OOV rate, and morphological boundary accuracy (MorphAcc). Our results show that BPE achieves the lowest fertility rate (1.636 at 16k vocab) by memorizing surface word forms, while achieving only 6.67% MorphAcc. PRPE achieves 83.33% MorphAcc - the highest of all systems - demonstrating that fertility rate alone is insufficient to evaluate tokenizers for agglutinative languages. All code and models are publicly available at kaggle.com/code/macmaky/quechuatok

</details>


### [84] [Layer-wise Probing of wav2vec 2.0 and Whisper for Consonant Cluster Reduction in African American English](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23948)
*Hamid Mojarad,Kevin Tang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper probes wav2vec2 and Whisper models on their encoding of consonant cluster reduction (CCR) in African American English (AAE). Findings reveal that the models encode CCR as structured phonological variation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to investigate how modern speech models encode the phenomenon of consonant cluster reduction (CCR) in African American English (AAE), given its impact on automatic speech recognition (ASR) disparities.

Method: The authors perform speaker-independent, layer-wise probing of two models—wav2vec2-base and Whisper-small. They use two tasks: segmental reduction detection and segmental restoration of the underlying cluster.

Result: Both models distinguished between reduced and canonical speech forms with high accuracy. Moreover, reduced segments revealed cues to their underlying consonants, indicating structured encoding of phonological variation.

Conclusion: The study concludes that CCR in African American English is encoded by speech models not as simple segmental deletion but as structured gradient phonological variation, evidencing the models' capacity for nuanced linguistic pattern representation.

Abstract: Self-supervised and supervised speech models are increasingly used to investigate which linguistic information their internal representations encode, and at what level of abstraction they encode it. One underexplored phenomenon is consonant cluster reduction (CCR) in African American English (AAE), a widespread phonological process and a source of automatic speech recognition (ASR) disparity. To examine how CCR is represented, we conduct speaker-independent layer-wise probing of wav2vec2-base and Whisper-small using two tasks: segmental reduction detection and segmental restoration of underlying cluster identity. Both models distinguish reduced and canonical forms with high accuracy. Crucially, reduced segments retain cues to their underlying stops, indicating that CCR is encoded as structured gradient phonological variation rather than simple segmental deletion. These results demonstrate structured phonological encoding of AAE CCR patterns in modern speech models.

</details>


### [85] [Does My Embedding Reflect That $A = B$? Evaluating Mathematical Equivalence in Embedding Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23959)
*Jiaying Ye,Samarth Rao,Leo Carlin,Kedar Chintalapati,Saharsh Bhargava,Rachit Jaiswal,Michael Zhou,Jared Darlington,Jarod Alper,Vasily Ilin,Henry Kvinge*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates the ability of embedding models to capture mathematical equivalence, introducing the MELD dataset and proposing a contrastive learning method to improve mathematical text embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge in mathematics arises from the highly abstract nature of the field, where the same concept can appear in vastly different forms across subfields, making cross-field navigation and understanding difficult.

Method: The authors created the MELD dataset of mathematically equivalent but lexically different statements and evaluated state-of-the-art embedding models. They proposed a contrastive learning approach to enhance embeddings by aligning informal statements with their formal versions.

Result: The proposed contrastive method improved model performance on informal-formal retrieval tasks and on evaluating mathematical equivalence using the MELD dataset.

Conclusion: Current models lack sufficient understanding of mathematical equivalence, but a contrastive learning framework offers a promising solution to bridge the gap.

Abstract: Because mathematics is highly abstract, a single statement can take very different forms depending on what subfield it is framed in. There are many examples where breakthroughs occurred after researchers discovered that a question had already been answered in a different field. At the same time, the growth of new resources related to formalization has increased the need for tools that enable efficient and reliable navigation between mathematical 'languages' (e.g., from Lean to natural language). In this paper, we investigate whether current embedding models capture mathematical equivalence. To do this, we introduce the Mathematically Equivalent but Lexically Different Pairs (MELD) Dataset, a collection of mathematically equivalent statements that are expressed in very different language. We show that current state-of-the-art embedding models tend to group statements by the terminology used to make them instead of the underlying math. Motivated by this, we propose a contrastive approach to learning embeddings of mathematical text that focuses on aligning informal statements with different formalizations. Our experiments demonstrate that this leads to improvements not only on informal-formal retrieval tasks but also on MELD, which only contains natural language statements.

</details>


### [86] [Faithful by Construction: Claim-Anchored Attribution for Multi-Document Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23989)
*Shuo Guan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CAMS is an attribution-focused modular framework for multi-document summarization, improving faithfulness and source traceability while balancing coverage and factual accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address hallucination and weak source attribution in large language model (LLM)-generated summaries by introducing a method that provides fine-grained, multi-source traceability and ensures better factual reliability.

Method: A modular pipeline named CAMS, which includes extracting atomic claims with fine-grained provenance, clustering and selecting support-aware claims, and rewriting these into summaries anchored to well-supported claims with token-level traceability.

Result: CAMS demonstrates improved faithfulness and citation precision over baselines while maintaining quality. It boosts attribution accuracy by up to two-thirds and introduces a controllable trade-off between faithfulness and coverage.

Conclusion: CAMS structurally enhances multi-document summarization by offering high traceability and factual faithfulness, providing a more reliable and transparent alternative to end-to-end LLMs.

Abstract: End-to-end large language models (LLMs) produce fluent multi-document summaries but remain prone to hallucination, and the attributions they offer are typically coarse (whole documents or passages) and generated post hoc, leaving each summary statement hard to verify. We revisit the modular Extract--Select--Rewrite paradigm and recast its intermediate representation as the unit of attribution. We present CAMS, a Claim-Anchored Multi-document Summarization framework that (i) extracts atomic claims with token-level provenance from every source document, (ii) clusters equivalent claims across documents while flagging inter-source conflicts, (iii) selects a support-aware and salient subset, and (iv) rewrites the selection into a summary in which every sentence is anchored to a support-checked claim that links back to one or more source spans. Because content is localized before it is realized, the pipeline is attribution-oriented by construction and faithfulness-oriented by construction: it structurally preserves fine-grained, multi-source traceability while using support-aware selection, constrained rewriting, and verification to encourage, rather than guarantee, factual faithfulness. We evaluate quality, faithfulness, and localization on MultiNews, analyze conflict handling on DiverseSumm, and test zero-shot transfer on WCEP, using a two-regime protocol that separates reference-free citation quality from gold-aligned localization accuracy, and we add an evaluator-decoupled audit that tests citation precision with a support model never used for selection or verification. CAMS matches strong end-to-end and span-attribution baselines on summary quality while substantially improving faithfulness and citation precision, lifting multi-source attribution accuracy by roughly two-thirds, and exposing a controllable faithfulness--coverage trade-off that end-to-end models leave implicit.

</details>


### [87] [RASC+: Retrieval-Constrained LLM Adjudication for Clinical Value Set Authoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23992)
*Sumit Mukherjee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses the inefficiency of zero-shot large language model (LLM) generation in clinical value set completion by proposing a two-stage approach combining high-recall candidate pool retrieval with LLM adjudication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improving clinical value set completion, critical for quality measurement and decision support, given that direct LLM generation struggles with the complexity and size of clinical code systems.

Method: A stage-wise methodology: first, optimizing candidate-pool retrieval for recall, and second, applying large language model (LLM) adjudication (e.g., GPT-5) for selection from this pool.

Result: Retrieval optimization raised candidate-pool recall from 0.553 to 0.730. Using blinded GPT-5 adjudication increased full-test macro F1 metric performance from 0.287 to 0.549, showing significant improvement.

Conclusion: Retrieval-constrained LLM adjudication effectively enhances clinical value set completion while ensuring adherence to safety constraints through an auditable candidate pool.

Abstract: Clinical value sets define the standardized terminology codes used in quality measurement, phenotyping, cohort construction, and clinical decision support. The recently introduced Retrieval-Augmented Set Completion (RASC) benchmark showed that direct zero-shot large language model (LLM) generation is poorly suited to this task: clinical code systems are large, version-controlled, and not reliably memorized by language models. We study a stage-wise alternative in which candidate-pool construction is optimized for recall and a constrained LLM adjudicator is optimized for candidate selection. On the full 3,744-value-set RASC test split, Qwen3-based retrieval with vocabulary-aware expansion and code-display rescue retrieval increases candidate-pool recall from the original RASC retrieval baseline of 0.553 to 0.730; on the held-out-publisher stratum, pool recall is 0.655. The higher-recall pool alone is not sufficient: applying the original SAPBert cross-encoder to this expanded pool gives full-test macro F1 of 0.287 and held-out-publisher macro F1 of 0.233. Replacing the stage-2 selector with blinded GPT-5 adjudication over the same pool increases full-test macro F1 to 0.549 and held-out-publisher macro F1 to 0.533. These results show that retrieval-constrained LLM adjudication can substantially improve value set completion while preserving the safety constraint that all returned codes must come from an auditable candidate pool.

</details>


### [88] [Towards Spec Learning: Inference-Time Alignment from Preference Pairs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24004)
*Dhriti Krishnan,Tejas Goyal,Jaromir Savelka*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces spec learning, a method to steer large language models using brief instructions and preferences without fine-tuning the model parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of steering LLMs effectively without relying on expensive fine-tuning or fragile manual prompt engineering processes.

Method: Spec learning involves compiling user instructions and preference judgments into natural-language specifications that condition the model's inference without updating its parameters.

Result: The paper demonstrates that the specifications generated through this method outperform direct preference optimization in dense preference signal datasets and provide interpretable, human-readable outputs.

Conclusion: Spec learning offers a practical and transparent alternative to fine-tuning, enabling efficient and interpretable adjustment of LLM behavior based on user preferences.

Abstract: Steering a large language model (LLM) toward a desired behavior typically relies on an iterative process of hand-crafting a prompt based on a careful inspection of the model's responses. This is an involved, brittle, and error-prone process. Preference-based fine-tuning is a more rigorous but often prohibitively expensive solution. We propose spec learning, a framework that relies on a brief user instruction and a small set of preference judgments. These are compiled into specifications in the form of natural-language prompts for an LLM. Specifications condition LLMs at inference time, and no parameter updates to the underlying models are required. We show that the responses generated based on the compiled specifications often outperform direct preference optimization (DPO) on datasets from specialized domains whose preference signal is dense. Unlike opaque weight updates, the resulting specifications are human-readable and double as interpretable and transparent written embodiments of the preference signal that produced them.

</details>


### [89] [Towards Version-aware Operations and Transaction Memories for Multi-layer MeMo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24040)
*Peiran Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces MeMo, a language model using explicit multi-layer correlation matrix memories (CMMs) for more flexible knowledge updating. It allows for local knowledge edits without retraining the entire model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to reduce the need for retraining language models when knowledge changes or updates are required. By enabling explicit memory management, the paper seeks to make updates more efficient and precise.

Method: The paper proposes explicit multi-layer correlation matrix memories (CMMs) with operations for memorization, retrieval, and forgetting. It includes a version-aware operation layer for handling complex update tasks like rollback and traceability using auxiliary CMMs for version transitions and reusable change tracking.

Result: The framework supports both sequence-level edits and structured diff-level updates, providing an effective route for evaluating update accuracy, rollback, traceability, and other metrics.

Conclusion: MeMo demonstrates the potential for language models to manage updates more efficiently by allowing knowledge edits at a granular level. This could minimize retraining demands and enhance model adaptability.
