Conclusion: Multimodal communication strategies are better suited for effective robot-to-human interactions, though real-world evaluations indicate reduced legibility and confidence, highlighting challenges in practical applications.

Abstract: Effective robot-to-human communication can increase transparency and trust, reduce uncertainty, and contribute to safer collaboration in shared workspaces. Designing and validating an effective robot communication strategy is challenging due to the varying and often limited communication modalities across robots, differences in how diverse recipients interpret messages, and the underexplored virtual-to-real gap in studies of communication legibility. We present a systematic, large-scale comparative validation of existing communication strategies for a mobile non-humanoid robot across message types and settings (online and in-person). Based on the prescribed message types in the existing standards for industrial robots, we realize and compare a low-expressive, unimodal LED-based strategy with a highly expressive, multimodal one that leverages robotic gaze, gestures, and voice. For each strategy, we analyze the communication of a turning intention, an attention request, error status, whether the robot is stuck, and whether it is functioning normally. We evaluate these strategies in replicated online and in-person experiments. We find strong evidence that highly expressive multimodal communication is perceived as more legible and intuitive than unimodal LED-based communication. Comparing the online and real-world study findings, we observe a notable decrease in overall legibility, particularly for signaling with LEDs. Similarly, confidence in message interpretation decreases during the real-world evaluation.

</details>


### [349] [Supervise What Survives: Geometry-Guided VLA Adaptation from Synthetic Robot Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24448)
*Danze Chen,Yanzhe Chen,Qiming Huang,Zhijun Cao,Chen Gao,Mike Zheng Shou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method to address the challenge of training Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robots using generated videos by focusing on geometric information instead of pseudo-actions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models depend on large-scale real robot demonstrations, which are scarce. Generated videos offer an alternative, but extracting low-level control (actions) from these visuals creates mismatches in abstraction.

Method: The authors propose GRA (Geometry-guided Representation Alignment), which utilizes geometric data (e.g., 2D end-effector waypoints) from human videos to train the visual system of VLA models while relying on real demonstrations solely for control signals.

Result: The proposed GRA approach outperforms methods relying on pseudo-actions when the data budget is matched. It also reduces the performance gap compared to systems trained with significantly more real robot demonstrations.

Conclusion: Geometric guidance, rather than pseudo-action recovery, offers a more reliable path from generated videos to robotic policies, validating the Asymmetric Preservation Principle described in the paper.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models require large-scale video-action pairs, yet real teleoperation remains scarce. While generated robot videos offer a scalable alternative, existing methods treat them as real robot data by recovering pseudo-actions from synthesized pixels. We argue that deriving low-level control from generated visuals is a mismatched abstraction. A video captures only \emph{geometry}: the spatial trajectory representing the \emph{where} of a task. A real demonstration captures \emph{control}: the exact motor commands representing the \emph{how}. Human-to-robot video generation preserves these unequally: the visible geometry survives the generation process, while the underlying control signals are lost. This \textbf{Asymmetric Preservation Principle} dictates a clean rule: this surviving geometry should solely supervise visual perception, leaving control to real demonstrations. Following this principle, we propose \textbf{GRA} (\textbf{G}eometry-guided \textbf{R}epresentation \textbf{A}lignment), which extracts the geometric content as future 2D end-effector waypoints, computed from the source human video through pose estimation, retargeting, simulation, and calibrated projection, and routes them to the VLA vision backbone via an auxiliary 2D head. The action head is trained on real demonstrations only. During fine-tuning, the waypoint loss persists as a \textbf{spatial representation anchor} that prevents the backbone from losing its geometric grounding. On real-robot tasks, GRA outperforms pseudo-action baselines under matched data budgets and narrows the gap to policies trained with substantially more real demonstrations, suggesting that correctly routed geometry bridges generated videos to robot policies more reliably than recovered actions.

</details>


### [350] [NoContactNoWorries: Estimating Contact through Vision and Proprioception for In-Hand Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24450)
*Soham Patil,Avirup Das,Sourabh Bhosale,Spandan Roy*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper develops a framework that uses vision and robot proprioception to infer binary contact states in robot manipulation without requiring tactile sensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots face challenges in relying on cost-intensive, fragile, and difficult-to-integrate tactile sensors for binary contact estimation, which motivates exploring whether visual input combined with proprioception can substitute these sensors, just as humans integrate such information for contact perception.

Method: The authors propose 'NoContactNoWorries,' a transformer-based multimodal framework that combines RGB-D visual data and robot proprioception to infer binary contact states. They evaluate the model's contact prediction on multiple objects and its utility in reinforcement learning tasks.

Result: The experiments demonstrate effective contact inference from vision and proprioception, exhibiting generalization to novel objects in both simulated and real-world environments. The inferred contact signals further enhance downstream robot capabilities such as in-hand object manipulation.

Conclusion: The study concludes that robots can leverage a vision-based approach to infer pseudo-tactile contact states, providing a viable and scalable alternative to hardware tactile sensors for improving robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Perceiving physical contact is fundamental to dexterous manipulation. While robots often rely on dedicated hardware tactile sensors, humans exhibit a remarkable ability to infer contact by integrating visual information with an innate sense of their body's pose and movement. Inspired by this embodied perceptual skill, we investigate whether a robot can learn to infer contact from vision, an approach that also offers a scalable alternative to tactile hardware specifically for binary contact estimation, which faces practical challenges in cost, fragility, and integration. We present NoContactNoWorries, a transformer-based multimodal framework that fuses RGB-D vision with the robot's proprioception to infer binary contact states as a pseudo-tactile signal for hand-object interactions. We validate by training a single contact prediction model on multiple objects and show that the inferred contact signal supports downstream reinforcement learning agents for in-hand object reorientation, generalizing to novel objects. Experiments in both simulation and on a real-world robot validate our approach, highlighting the feasibility of inferring contact from vision and proprioception. Project Page: https://soham2560.github.io/no-contact-no-worries/

</details>


### [351] [Varying Bundle Size Reactive Multi-Task Assignment using Selective Cost Estimation for Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24462)
*Niklas Dahlquist,Shridhar Velhal,George Nikolakopoulos*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a scalable two-stage multi-fidelity framework for multi-robot task allocation that balances computational efficiency and accurate cost estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing auction-based methods for multi-robot task allocation struggle with computationally expensive path planning and bundle generation, making them impractical for real-time applications.

Method: A two-stage multi-fidelity strategy where agents use low-fidelity heuristics to explore bundles and high-fidelity path planning for promising candidates; a coordinator then resolves a set packing problem to maximize utility.

Result: Simulation results show that the framework performs well across diverse environments, improving reactive auction-based task allocation with scalable bundle sizes.

Conclusion: The approach enables dynamic, scalable multi-robot task allocation by efficiently balancing exploration and computational costs, ensuring global feasibility and performance optimization.

Abstract: This paper presents a scalable framework for multi-robot task allocation in complex environments where estimating task execution costs is computationally expensive. While combinatorial auction-based approaches offer reliable solutions, the exponential complexity of bundle generation typically renders them intractable for real-time reactive applications, particularly when accurate path planning is required for cost validation. We address this through a distributed, two-stage multi-fidelity bundle generation approach. Agents utilize a local search tree guided by a low-fidelity heuristic (such as euclidean distance) to rapidly explore the bundle space, applying high-fidelity path planning only to the most promising candidates in a best-first manner. These refined bids are then submitted to a central coordinator that solves a set packing problem to ensure global feasibility and maximize the overall utility. Simulation results in multiple environments demonstrate that the framework is able to improve the performance of reactive auction-based task allocation. Overall, the presented framework is shown to enable reactive task allocation with dynamic bundle sizes in multiple settings without exposing the agents' state and internal cost estimation models.

</details>


### [352] [FT-WBC: Learning Fault-Tolerant Whole-Body Control for Legged Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24466)
*Yudong Zhong,Pengfei Mai,Sikai Guo,Jiahang Cao,Zhihai Bi,Qiuyue Liu,Ziyan Feng,Jinni Zhou,Jun Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces FT-WBC, a framework improving fault-tolerant stability and manipulation for legged robots in the face of actuator failures, validated through simulation and real-world testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of instability and task failure in legged manipulators under actuator failures due to Center-of-Mass shifts and dynamic disturbances caused by robotic arms.

Method: FT-WBC framework includes a Fault Estimator (FE) for detecting actuator failures and a Posture Adaptation Module (PAM) to adjust posture plans, enhancing fault-tolerant whole-body control.

Result: FT-WBC improves robot stability, workspace range, and survival rate during actuator failures in both simulations and real-world scenarios, with zero-shot transferability to real hardware.

Conclusion: FT-WBC effectively ensures fault tolerance for legged robots performing loco-manipulation tasks, maintaining stability and operational capability under actuator failures.

Abstract: Legged manipulators combine the mobility of legged platforms with the manipulation capability of robotic arms. However, arm-induced Center-of-Mass shifts and dynamic disturbances make the system more prone to instability under actuator failures, potentially leading to falls, task failures, or safety risks. Existing fault-tolerant control methods mainly focus on locomotion alone, leaving the coupled problem of whole-body stability and arm reachability in fault-tolerant loco-manipulation largely unaddressed. To bridge this gap, we propose FT-WBC, a fault-tolerant loco-manipulation framework for robust whole-body control of legged manipulators under actuator failures. FT-WBC adopts a decoupled upper- and lower-body policy architecture and introduces two key modules: a Fault Estimator (FE) and a Posture Adaptation Module (PAM). The FE predicts faulty joints from lower-body proprioceptive histories, while the PAM uses this fault information to adapt the base posture plan generated by the arm policy, converting potentially unstable posture requests into safe and executable base posture commands. Through this fault-aware posture adaptation mechanism, FT-WBC synthesizes compensatory gaits under actuator failures and preserves as much arm workspace as possible while maintaining whole-body stability. Simulation and real-world experiments show that FT-WBC significantly improves survival rate and workspace under weakening or locked failures, and transfers zero-shot to a real legged manipulator in the real world.

</details>


### [353] [G$^3$VLA: Geometric inductive bias for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24472)
*Yue Peng,Yongzhe Zhao,Artur Habuda,Khuyen Pham,Yanheng Zhu,Tran Nguyen Le,Fares Abu-Dakka,Li Guo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: G$^3$VLA enhances vision-language-action (VLA) models with a geometry-aware module for better robot manipulation, integrating calibrated camera knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the shortcomings of current VLA models, particularly their inability to fully utilize calibrated camera geometries, causing inefficiencies in multi-camera setups.

Method: The proposed G$^3$VLA introduces a geometry-aware module employing calibrated camera parameters, intrinsic-conditioned ray embeddings, projective positional encoding (PRoPE), and bidirectional cross-view fusion. It uses geometric supervision through either ground-truth point maps or a confidence-gated $π^3$X teacher.

Result: G$^3$VLA demonstrates consistent performance improvements on benchmarks like LIBERO, RoboCasa24, and real robot setups, excelling in spatially and object-sensitive tasks.

Conclusion: Injecting geometry-aware mechanisms in VLA models significantly boosts their ability to perform complex robotic tasks in various setups, proving the effectiveness of geometric transfer mechanisms.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have made rapid progress in generalist robot manipulation by harnessing semantic knowledge from pretrained vision-language backbones, but their visual tokens remain grounded in 2D image coordinates rather than the calibrated geometry of the robot's cameras -- a mismatch especially pronounced in multi-camera setups, where views are coupled by known intrinsics and extrinsics yet processed as independent images. We propose G$^3$VLA, a camera-aware geometric module that injects calibrated structure into the visual-token stream of a pretrained VLA without altering its action space or imitation objective, combining intrinsic-conditioned ray embeddings, projective positional encoding (PRoPE), and bidirectional cross-view fusion. Geometric supervision is provided either from ground-truth point maps when available, or from confidence-gated $π^3$X teacher predictions, requiring no depth sensors or manual annotations. Instantiated on $π_0$, G$^3$VLA yields consistent gains across the LIBERO suites, RoboCasa24, RoboTwin2.0, and real-robot settings, with the largest improvements on spatially and object-sensitive tasks. We further validate on $π_{0.5}$ and GR00T 1.5, with results suggesting that geometric transfer is most effective when geometry-aware tokens have direct access to the action generation pathway. Our project page is at https://sites.google.com/view/g3vla

</details>


### [354] [Decentralized Pose Graph Riemannian Optimization for Object-based Multi-Robot SLAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24489)
*Yixian Zhao,Yan Huang,Yang Xu,Liang Li,Jinming Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a decentralized Riemannian optimization framework for multi-robot Pose Graph Optimization (PGO) in SLAM, addressing challenges in sparse and variable communication scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current decentralized PGO solutions assume consistent and dense communication networks, which is unrealistic in dynamic, sparse, and intermittent communication conditions in real deployments.

Method: The authors developed a Riemannian optimization approach combined with a consensus mechanism to decouple object and trajectory estimation and introduced an approximate-Newton scheme for better convergence and efficiency on SE(d) manifolds.

Result: The approach improves iteration counts, reduces communication overhead, and maintains accuracy. Its effectiveness is validated through benchmarks, simulations, and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: The proposed method is scalable, efficient, robust to communication issues, and enhances estimation accuracy under decentralized multi-robot SLAM scenarios.

Abstract: Pose graph optimization (PGO) is a key back-end component for state estimation in networked multi-robot simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM). In object-based multi-robot SLAM, the problem becomes more tightly coupled because robots must jointly estimate both their trajectories and the poses of persistent objects observed by multiple agents. Existing decentralized solutions often assume that the communication graph closely matches the physical interaction topology, which is restrictive in realistic deployments where communication is sparse, intermittent, or time-varying. This paper presents a fully decentralized Riemannian optimization framework for object-based multi-robot PGO that decouples the coupled estimation problem via a consensus mechanism, enabling flexible communication topologies. To improve convergence under limited communication budgets, we further develop a distributed approximate-Newton scheme that exploits local second-order information while operating directly on the SE(d) manifold to preserve geometric consistency, and we establish the convergence to Riemannian first-order stationary points and provide a local condition-number analysis explaining the benefit of approximate second-order information over first-order Riemannian descent. The resulting method reduces iteration count and communication overhead without sacrificing estimation accuracy. Extensive evaluations on public benchmarks, large-scale simulations, and real-world multi-robot experiments demonstrate improved accuracy, runtime efficiency, scalability across network topologies, and robustness to communication failures.

</details>


### [355] [Explaining Failures of Cyber-Physical Systems with Actual Causality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24546)
*Khen Elimelech,Tom Yaacov,David A. Kelly,Hana Chockler,Moshe Y. Vardi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel approach using actual causality for explaining failures in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs), such as self-driving cars. It presents two explanation derivation algorithms and evaluates them in the context of a collision-avoidance system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of unexpected failures in autonomous CPSs due to their black-box nature, and to improve trust and post-failure mitigation by deriving explanations for such failures.

Method: The authors leverage the framework of actual causality for failure explanations in CPSs, bridging theoretical gaps to extend its use to complex systems. Additionally, they propose two novel algorithms for explanation derivation—prioritizing either optimality or efficiency.

Result: The proposed approach and algorithms are demonstrated and evaluated using a neural-network-controlled autonomous car for collision avoidance, showcasing their practical applicability.

Conclusion: The paper contributes a theoretical extension and practical tools for failure explanation in CPSs, enhancing system reliability and trustworthiness through causal analysis.

Abstract: Modern autonomous Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs), such as self-driving cars, face increasingly complex demands, and yet are expected to act reliably. The black-box nature often characterizing such systems, especially those relying on neural components, makes it impossible to fully verify the system behavior prior to deployment. Unfortunately, unexpected failures-when the system does not comply with its specification-are inevitable and may have catastrophic implications. To improve trust in the system and facilitate future mitigation after a failure occurs, it is important to try to derive an explanation for the unexpected system behavior. This paper introduces the novel concept of leveraging the framework of actual causality for CPS failure explanation. Up until now, this framework was only used to derive explanations in the context of simple systems, such as image classifiers. This paper addresses the theoretical gaps and provides the guidance needed to allow for correct explanation derivation in the CPS domain. Beyond the theoretical contribution, the paper presents two novel, practical, system-agnostic explanation derivation algorithms, allowing to prioritize either explanation optimality or derivation efficiency. The approach is demonstrated and evaluated in the context of a neural-network-controlled autonomous car, designed to avoid collisions.

</details>


### [356] [Enabling Robust Cloth Manipulation via Inference-Time Simulator-in-the-Loop Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24552)
*Xin Liu,Yulin Li,Ziming Li,Pengyu Jing,Zhenhao Huang,Bingyang Zhou,Ziqiu Zeng,Siyuan Luo,Chenkun Qi,Fan Shi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a framework for robot cloth manipulation using a simulator-in-the-loop optimization approach that combines synthetic data generation, visual mapping from RGB to cloth state, and online planning using simulation rollouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to adapt simulator-in-the-loop optimization, proven effective in rigid-body manipulation, to the challenging domain of deformable cloth manipulation using a single RGB input.

Method: The method integrates three components: a synthetic-data generation pipeline using FLASH simulator, a real-to-sim module that maps RGB observations to cloth state, and an online planning system using sparse-mesh rollout and model predictive path integral (MPPI) control.

Result: Experiments on real robots demonstrate superior success rates and robustness compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: The proposed approach successfully enables scalable and robust cloth manipulation in real-world settings by leveraging synthetic simulation data, visual perception mapping, and efficient rollout-based planning.

Abstract: Simulator-in-the-loop optimization offers a promising inference-time mechanism for robot manipulation. It uses a physical simulator as a backend rollout engine to evaluate candidate trajectories in parallel and refine nominal actions online, a paradigm proven effective in rigid-body manipulation where state and contact are relatively tractable. We bring this paradigm to real-world cloth manipulation from a single RGB input through three pillars. (i) We design a scalable synthetic-data generation and inference-time rollout pipeline built on FLASH, a deformable-object simulator that provides a practical balance among physical fidelity, numerical stability, and rollout efficiency. (ii) We develop a real-to-sim module, trained purely on synthetic data, that maps a single RGB observation to simulation-compatible cloth state by fusing pretrained visual features with learnable canonical tokens. (iii) We perform online planning by coupling a sparse-mesh rollout backend with prior-guided MPPI, anchored at an offline-distilled policy trajectory, preserving manipulation-relevant deformation and contact while enabling sufficient parallel rollout batches. Real-robot experiments show higher success rates and stronger robustness than baseline methods.

</details>


### [357] [ArtiTwinSplat: Interactable Digital Twin Reconstruction via Gaussian Splatting from RGB-D videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24628)
*Pranjal Mishra,René Zurbrügg,Max Wilder-Smith,Marco Hutter,Marc Pollefeys,Zuria Bauer,Hermann Blum*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ArtiTwinSplat is a framework that creates digital twins of objects from RGB-D videos, focusing on articulation discovery and realistic 3D modeling without needing prior resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic systems require accurate object models for real-world deployment, but creating these at scale is challenging.

Method: Uses 3D Gaussian Splatting and unsupervised articulation discovery from RGB-D videos, paired with tracking and optimization for interactive, realistic digital twins.

Result: Produces photorealistic, articulated digital twins usable for robot planning and human-robot collaboration directly from real-world observations.

Conclusion: ArtiTwinSplat makes scalable, realistic modeling feasible, lowering robotic system integration barriers and advancing embodied AI applications.

Abstract: Deploying robots in unstructured real-world environments needs accurate, interactive models of the objects. Constructing these models at scale remains a critical bottleneck for robotic system integration. We present ArtiTwinSplat, a framework that automatically constructs articulated, photo-realistic digital twins of objects directly from RGB-D videos, requiring no CAD models, simulation assets, or manual annotations. Our method is built on 3D Gaussian Splatting that preserve geometric fidelity and photometric realism, coupled with an unsupervised articulation discovery pipeline that recovers part structure and joint kinematics from observed motion alone. With tracking and optimization stages our method provides stable, queryable digital twins that support real-time rendering, viewpoint control, and interactive manipulation. Unlike prior methods confined to simulation, ArtiTwinSplat operates directly on real-world observations and produces twins that are immediately usable by downstream robot planning and learning systems. This method offers a practical, scalable pathway toward digital twin construction, lowering the integration barrier for articulated object manipulation in embodied AI and human-robot collaboration contexts.

</details>


### [358] [Optimization-based Safe Trajectory Planning for Autonomous Ground Vehicle in Multi-Floor Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24631)
*Zishang Xiang,Runda Zhang,Runqi Chai,Kaiyuan Chen,Senchun Chai,Yuanqing Xia*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a trajectory planning framework for AGVs in multi-floor environments, utilizing task and trajectory planning modules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the challenge of navigating autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs) efficiently in multi-floor environments.

Method: Task planning utilizes generalized voronoi diagrams and multi-objective algorithms, while trajectory planning employs optimization-based methods with a hierarchical planning framework and a correlation constraint calculation.

Result: The proposed framework is demonstrated to be effective and feasible through simulation experiments.

Conclusion: The paper validates the framework's ability to handle multi-floor trajectory planning for AGVs effectively.

Abstract: The development of trajectory planning strategies for autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs) represents a prevailing research interest within the domain of intelligent transportation systems. This paper introduces a trajectory planning framework tailored for multi-floor scenarios. The framework consists of two main modules: the task planning module and the trajectory planning module. The task planning module involves a strategic selection phase, where a task planning strategy based on generalized voronoi diagrams (GVD) and multi-objective algorithms is proposed to select the floor exits for each floor. The trajectory planning module utilizes optimization-based methods to generate high-quality trajectories, and a warm-started hierarchical planning framework is designed to ensure rapid convergence. Additionally, for handling complex obstacle constraints, a correlation constraint calculation method is designed for reducing obstacle constraints in trajectory planning. Finally, the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed framework are verified through simulations.

</details>


### [359] [Beyond Monotonic Progress: Retry-Supervised Value Learning for Robot Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24633)
*Xinyao Qin,Junjie Lu,Kaixin Wang,Chuheng Zhang,Sinjae Kang,Kimin Lee,Min Xu,Bin Liang,Jun Yang,Li Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ReTVL is a novel framework that processes robot demonstrations containing both errors and corrective behaviors to enhance imitation learning by distinguishing valuable retries from harmful mistakes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many robot demonstrations for imitation learning include errors and corrective actions, traditionally labeled as noisy or poor-quality data, despite holding useful information about error correction and task recovery.

Method: The proposed ReTVL framework uses retry events as sparse supervision to create mistake-sensitive value functions, employing global task progress calibration and local pairwise preference learning to handle mixed-quality demonstrations.

Result: ReTVL delivers more precise value estimates than conventional progress-based models and enhances the quality of imitation learning in real-world robot manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: Incorporating retries as learning signals allows ReTVL to reduce the negative impact of demonstration errors while retaining the corrective elements, leading to improved robot performance in complex tasks.

Abstract: Human demonstrations for robot imitation learning often contain mistakes and corrective behaviors, such as imprecise grasps, object misalignment, unstable contact, and repeated attempts. While these segments are commonly treated as noisy or suboptimal data, they provide valuable evidence about when execution deviates from a desirable path and how task feasibility can be restored. However, existing reward and value models often rely on monotonic progress assumptions, which capture coarse task advancement but may overlook local execution errors and corrective behaviors in imperfect demonstrations. In this work, we propose ReTVL (ReTry-Supervised Value Learning), a framework for learning mistake-sensitive value functions from mixed-quality robot demonstrations by leveraging retry events as sparse supervision. ReTVL captures the local degradation-and-recovery structure around mistakes by combining global progress calibration with local pairwise preference learning induced by sparsely annotated retry keypoints. The learned value model is then used to reweight demonstration chunks for downstream behavior cloning, reducing the influence of harmful execution errors while preserving useful corrective behaviors. Experiments on real-robot manipulation tasks show that ReTVL produces more fine-grained value estimates than progress-based baselines and improves imitation learning from imperfect demonstrations.

</details>


### [360] [TACTFUL: Tactile-Driven Exploration For Object Localization and Identification in Confined Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24712)
*Shivani Kamtikar,Chung Hee Kim,Camilla Tabasso,Tye Brady,Joshua Migdal,Taskin Padir*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces TACTFUL, a framework allowing robots to explore using touch rather than vision, achieving high success in object identification and reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans can locate and identify objects through touch alone. Robotic systems, however, rely heavily on vision, which limits their efficiency in scenarios needing tactile exploration.

Method: TACTFUL trains a framework for tactile exploration using a multi-fingered robot without vision. It uses a learning policy that balances workspace exploration and surface refinement with dynamic rewards, entirely trained on hardware.

Result: TACTFUL achieves 77% success in object identification tasks and an average error of 0.015 m in tactile reconstruction, outperforming existing methods on real objects.

Conclusion: Tactile sensing, combined with structured learning, can effectively replace vision-based systems for object reasoning, making robots more autonomous in confined spaces and complex tasks.

Abstract: Humans effortlessly locate and identify objects by touch alone, even without vision. In contrast, robotic systems rely heavily on vision and struggle with autonomous tactile exploration and object identification. We present TACTFUL, a vision-free tactile exploration framework that enables a multi-fingered robot to autonomously explore confined workspaces, discover objects through contact, and identify them via tactile reconstruction. Trained entirely on real hardware without simulation, our system learns a single policy that balances global workspace exploration with local surface refinement through a dynamic reward schedule. Our results demonstrate that tactile sensing, when paired with structured learning, can serve as an effective primary modality for object-level reasoning, achieving 77% success with 0.015 m average reconstruction error and outperforming baseline approaches on real-world objects.

</details>


### [361] [World Value Models for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24742)
*Zhihao Wang,Jianxiong Li,Yu Cui,Yuan Gao,Xianyuan Zhan,Junzhi Yu,Xiao Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new robotic value model, World Value Model (WVM), which uses world models for improved temporal understanding and value estimation. WVM achieves state-of-the-art performance in benchmarks and demonstrates robustness with both expert and suboptimal data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most existing robotic value models rely on Vision-Language Model (VLM) backbones that lack adequate temporal modeling capabilities. This limitation hinders accurate value estimation required for scaling robotic policy learning from mixed-quality data.

Method: The authors integrate world models—known for their temporal modeling and future planning capabilities—with value estimation to create the World Value Model (WVM). To evaluate WVM, they also propose a novel Suboptimal-Value-Bench consisting of 800 suboptimal trajectories.

Result: WVM delivers state-of-the-art Value-Order Correlation (VOC) results on standard benchmarks. It also excels in the new Suboptimal-Value-Bench and demonstrates improved manipulation performance during simulated and real-world policy deployments.

Conclusion: By leveraging world models, WVM offers robust and accurate value estimations, proving effective in learning from mixed-quality data. It establishes itself as a generalist, reliable model for robotic value estimation, surpassing previous approaches.

Abstract: Generalist value models play a pivotal role in scaling robotic policy learning from large-scale, mixed-quality data. Mathematically, accurate value estimation demands deep temporal understanding, requiring models to both ground the current belief using historical context and plan over future outcomes. However, most existing robotic value models are built on Vision-Language Model (VLM) backbones that are pretrained primarily on static or temporally sparse visual observations, lacking the requisite temporal modeling capabilities for value estimation. Unlike VLMs, world models naturally excel at temporal modeling and future planning, making them ideal foundations for learning generalizable value functions. Driven by this insight, we marry world models with value estimation to construct a new generalist robotic value model, World Value Model (WVM), that offers accurate task progressions to assess data quality. On standard benchmarks, WVM delivers state-of-the-art (SOTA) Value-Order Correlation (VOC) results. Complementing standard evaluation suites that contains only expert data, we further introduce Suboptimal-Value-Bench, a multi-embodiment benchmark consisting of 800 suboptimal trajectories with high-fidelity, human-labeled frame annotations. Our evaluations show that WVM maintains its SOTA performance on Suboptimal-Value-Bench, establishing its robustness in handling both expert and suboptimal data. When deployed for policy learning, WVM improves manipulation performance across various policy extraction approaches in both simulated and real-world deployment, providing robust guidance for learning from mixed-quality data.

</details>


### [362] [Vision-Language Model Reasoning for Contextual Semantic Mapping in Intralogistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24814)
*Marvin Rüdt,Hao Pang,Constantin Enke,Zäzilia Seibold,Kai Furmans*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a pipeline that uses SLAM, SAM, VLM, and clustering to create contextual semantic maps for mobile robots in intralogistics environments, achieving high accuracy in contextual understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current mobile robots lack semantic and contextual understanding in addition to geometric localization and navigation in dynamic environments.

Method: The pipeline combines SLAM-based mapping, SAM-based segmentation, instance clustering, and VLM-based reasoning in a zero-shot setting to infer properties like movability without task-specific training.

Result: Achieved 98.93% mIoU for semantic classification and 89.17% mAcc for movability estimation. VLM reasoning and clustering were identified as limitations.

Conclusion: The contextual semantic mapping pipeline enhances filtering and navigation capabilities in dynamic environments but has challenges in VLM reasoning and clustering performance.

Abstract: Autonomous mobile robots operating in intralogistics environments rely on geometric maps for localization and navigation, but lack semantic understanding of objects and their contextual properties. We present a contextual semantic mapping pipeline that combines SLAM-based geometric mapping, SAM-based instance segmentation, instance clustering, and VLM multi-view reasoning to produce a contextual semantic map representation encoding geometric structure, object class, and object movability. By aggregating observations across multiple viewpoints and querying a VLM in a zero-shot, open-vocabulary setting, the pipeline infers contextual object properties--here demonstrated through movability--without requiring task-specific training or predefined object categories. We evaluate three VLMs under two prompting strategies and conduct a component-wise analysis of the pipeline. The proposed pipeline achieves 98.93 % mIoU for semantic classification and 89.17 % mAcc for object movability estimation. Component analysis identifies VLM reasoning as the primary bottleneck for contextual understanding and instance clustering as the main limitation for panoptic performance. The resulting semantic map supports context-aware filtering and robust navigation in dynamic intralogistics environments.

</details>


### [363] [InSight: Self-Guided Skill Acquisition via Steerable VLAs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24884)
*Maggie Wang,Lars Osterberg,Stephen Tian,Ola Shorinwa,Jiajun Wu,Mac Schwager*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: InSight is a framework that enhances vision-language-action (VLA) models by allowing autonomous skill acquisition through primitive-action-level steerability and continual learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional VLA models' capabilities are limited by the skills in their training data, necessitating research into autonomous skill acquisition for broader applicability.

Method: The InSight framework comprises automated segmentation of demonstrations to primitive actions for steerability and uses a VLM-guided feedback loop to identify and incorporate new primitives continuously into the training dataset.

Result: InSight enables VLA models to autonomously learn manipulation skills like block flipping and pouring in both simulated and real-world settings without requiring prior human demonstrations for these tasks.

Conclusion: Primitive-action-level steerability and continual skill acquisition in VLAs support the development of adaptable systems capable of novel tasks without extensive human supervision.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models can learn manipulation skills from demonstrations, but their capabilities are bounded by the skills in the training data. We present InSight, a framework that unlocks autonomous skill acquisition by rendering VLAs steerable at the primitive-action level (e.g., "move gripper to the bowl", "lift upward", "pour the bottle"). InSight consists of two primary stages: (1) an automated segmentation pipeline that partitions demonstrations into labeled primitives via VLM plan decomposition and end-effector poses to enable VLA primitive steerability, and (2) a VLM-guided data flywheel that identifies missing primitives required to accomplish a novel task, autonomously attempts demonstrations of the missing primitives with VLM-proposed low-level control, and automatically labels, stores, and integrates successful demonstrations into the VLA training set. We evaluate InSight across simulation and real-world manipulation tasks, including block flipping, drawer closing, sweeping, twisting, and pouring, without any human demonstrations of these target skills. Once learned, these primitives can be composed to execute novel, long-horizon tasks without additional human demonstrations. Our findings demonstrate that primitive steerability provides a practical foundation for continual skill acquisition in VLA policies. Project website: https://insight-vla.github.io.

</details>


<div id='cs.SE'></div>

# cs.SE [[Back]](#toc)

### [364] [Beyond the Autoregressive Horizon: A Comprehensive Survey of Diffusion Models, World Modelling, and State Space Models for Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23690)
*Kishan Maharaj,Ashita Saxena,Srikanth Tamilselvam*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Current autoregressive (AR) language models have limitations in code reasoning. This paper explores novel paradigms like Diffusion Models, Code World Models (CWMs), and State Space Models (SSMs) to address these issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the structural and logical shortcomings in code reasoning posed by the next-token prediction paradigm of current AR language models.

Method: The paper evaluates and discusses emerging architectural paradigms such as Diffusion Models for holistic denoising, CWMs for execution-state simulation, and SSMs for efficient handling of large contexts.

Result: These paradigms potentially address limitations of AR models, such as maintaining long-range dependencies, more efficient scaling, and better grounding in program semantics.

Conclusion: By proposing novel paradigms and exploring their connections to cognitive neuroscience, a pathway toward next-generation code reasoning and "System 2" code generation agents is outlined.

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) language models have driven significant progress in automated software engineering, enabling powerful code generation and assistance systems. However, the next-token prediction paradigm introduces structural limitations for code reasoning, including restricted global planning, challenges in maintaining long-range dependencies, and limited grounding in program execution semantics. Noting the heavy skewness of existing literature towards AR models, we discuss emerging paradigms that could potentially overcome the logic and scaling bottlenecks of next-token prediction by unlocking next-generation architectural capabilities for code intelligence. Specifically, we discuss the potential of Diffusion Models, which generate code via holistic denoising that captures long-range syntactic constraints often missed by AR models. We also discuss Code World Models (CWMs), which simulate execution states to support reasoning, and State Space Models (SSMs), which provide linear-time efficiency for massive contexts. By connecting these developments with findings from cognitive neuroscience, we outline directions for developing "System 2" code generation agents.

</details>


### [365] [From Heuristics to Transformers: A Comprehensive Survey of Type Inference from Stripped Binaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23692)
*Hua Zheng,Yuhang Guo,Kuanishbay Sadatdiynov,Cheng Wen,Muhammad Sadiq,Dugang Liu,Jawwad Ahmed Shamsi,Anam Qureshi*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper surveys the evolution of binary type inference techniques from early heuristics to state-of-the-art machine learning models, highlighting challenges and future directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand and enhance techniques for recovering high-level type information from stripped binaries, crucial for reverse engineering, vulnerability analysis, and decompilation.

Method: The paper reviews various approaches, from rule-based and static analysis techniques to advanced neural and graph-based models, and identifies shifts in methodologies over time.

Result: It provides a taxonomy of existing solutions, tracks the transition from traditional techniques to neural models, and discusses the state-of-the-art in Transformer and GNN architectures.

Conclusion: Key challenges like semantics loss and structural type recovery are identified, and future research directions in neuro-symbolic inference are proposed.

Abstract: The recovery of high-level type information from stripped binaries-executables devoid of symbol tables and debugging information-is a cornerstone of software reverse engineering, vulnerability analysis, and decompilation. This survey tracks the evolution of binary type inference from early rule-based heuristics and static analysis to modern deep learning architectures. We analyze the shift from "duck typing" and constraint-solving techniques (e.g., BITY, BinSub) to context-aware neural models (e.g., EKLAVYA, CATI) and finally to state-of-the-art Transformer and Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures (e.g., SeeType, TYGR). We identify core challenges, including optimization-induced semantics loss and structural type recovery, and propose future research directions in neuro-symbolic inference.

</details>


### [366] [SemChunk-C: Semantic Segmentation for C Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23697)
*Boris Nazarov,Darya Frolova,Shaked Leibzirer,Pavel Kisilev*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces SemChunk-C, a lightweight LLM-based method for semantic code chunking in C-family languages, addressing challenges in syntax complexity and macro handling, and outperforms prior chunkers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chunking code in C-family languages remains problematic due to the complexity of syntax, macro expansions, and irregular patterns, which impacts LLM-driven downstream tasks.

Method: SemChunk-C uses a combination of LLM classifiers to identify chunk boundaries and assign functional attributes, leveraging boundary flexibility for better semantic understanding. It employs lightweight Ettin encoder variants.

Result: SemChunk-C achieves high accuracy and semantic coherence in chunking, surpassing larger code-oriented LLMs, and demonstrates improved downstream LLM task performance on benchmarks.

Conclusion: SemChunk-C effectively addresses semantic chunking challenges, providing a robust lightweight solution relevant for C-related languages and enabling better downstream applications.

Abstract: Semantic segmentation of code written in a C-family language remains a challenging problem, due to the language's complex syntax, macro expansion, and irregular structural patterns. Existing chunking methods, such as fixed-sized windows, heuristic splitting, and syntax-based tools, often fail to capture meaningful functional units, limiting the efficacy of retrieval and other downstream LLM driven tasks.
  In this paper, we address the problem of chunking in C-related languages. First, we define a set of code chunk categories. Second, we train an LLM-based classifier to a) identify chunk boundaries, and b) assign each chunk a descriptive functional attribute (a category), which can be useful for downstream tasks. By leveraging the LLM's ability to capture semantic context within the code, we assume flexible chunk boundaries, allowing to adapt to the specific structure and context of each instance. Third, we introduce SemChunk-C, a family of lightweight language models for semantic chunking of C-related files (.c, .cpp, .h, .cs, etc.). These models are based on the first four Ettin encoders [1] with 17M, 32M, 68M, and 150M parameters. Despite their relatively small size, they are capable of identifying cohesive code units, such as data structures, interface blocks, and other components. Furthermore, we demonstrate the robustness of our approach on real-world code, including challenging constructs such as nested definitions and macros.
  We test our approach on various datasets, and show that it achieves high boundary accuracy and semantic coherence, matching or outperforming chunkers that are based on much larger code-oriented LLMs. We also validate the improved performance of the downstream tasks on a few curated benchmarks.

</details>


### [367] [Privacy Engineering: A Systematic Literature Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23696)
*Nemania Borovits,Damian Andrew Tamburri,Willem-Jan van den Heuvel*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper reviews 90 studies on GDPR's impact on privacy engineering, identifying 13 key dimensions clustered into two cores (technology-focused and governance-focused). It maps lifecycle and domain-specific applications, highlighting future priorities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to synthesize existing research and chart the dimensions shaping privacy engineering under GDPR compliance.

Method: A systematic review coupled with thematic synthesis was conducted on studies from 2018-2025.

Result: The analysis reveals thematic clusters like Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs), Privacy Metrics (PM), Governance and Accountability (GA), and others, with lifecycle phases and domain-specific applications characterized.

Conclusion: The research provides a structured map for privacy engineering under GDPR, paving the way for practical application and further exploration in underrepresented areas.

Abstract: Privacy obligations under GDPR increasingly shape software engineering. We synthesize 90 studies from 2018 to 2025 using a systematic review with thematic synthesis to chart privacy engineering. Thirteen dimensions form two recurrent cores: Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs) with Privacy Metrics (PM) and Verification and Testing (VT) and Governance and Accountability (GA) with Transparency and Communication (TC) and Organizational Measures (OM). Modeling and Specification (MS) mediates between the cores. Lifecycle mapping shows concentrations at requirements and design (MS, GA), at implementation and verification (PETs, VT, PM, TC) and at operation and decommissioning (GA, OM, Data Subject Rights Management (DSRM), Incident Response and Management (IRM), Lifelong Management (LM)). Handoffs link models to rules and tests, mechanisms to metrics and deployments such as enclaves and ledgers to governance records. Domains reweight but do not alter structure: healthcare weights GA with VT and PETs, IoT and edge weight PETs with VT and PM at device and edge, web measurement weights TC with VT, AI and ML weight PETs with PM. IRM, LM and Data Minimization and Purpose Limitation (DMPL) are less often primary foci, signaling priorities for future work. The results provide a practical map and a replication-ready scaffold for assessment and updates.

</details>


### [368] [A Unified Framework for Runtime Verification and Model-Based Diagnosis in LOLA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23720)
*Raik Hipler,Martin Leucker,Patrick Rodler*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework that integrates runtime verification and model-based diagnosis using the LOLA stream specification language for continuous fault detection and localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing processes typically use separate tools for fault detection and diagnosis, which can lead to inefficiencies.

Method: The framework encodes system descriptions, health states, and observations into the LOLA stream specification language, facilitating simultaneous online fault detection and localization.

Result: The approach supports handling various fault types, including transient and nondeterministic scenarios, seamlessly within its unified structure.

Conclusion: Using LOLA unifies system monitoring processes, making fault detection and localization more efficient and streamlined.

Abstract: We present an integrated framework that unifies runtime verification and model-based diagnosis within the stream specification language LOLA. By encoding system descriptions, component health states, and observations into a single stream-based formalism, the approach enables continuous, online fault localization directly alongside fault detection, without requiring separate toolchains. The framework supports both time-invariant and transient faults, and naturally accommodates nondeterministic observations.

</details>


### [369] [ESAA-Conversational: An Event-Sourced Memory Layer for Continuity, Handoff, and Curation Across Heterogeneous LLM Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23752)
*Elzo Brito dos Santos Filho*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces ESAA-Conversational, a shared conversational memory system for coding agents like Codex and Claude Code, addressing the problem of state drift among heterogeneous LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The problem arises from developers using multiple LLM coding agents, each with a private log, leading to unshared states and inefficiencies when switching between agents.

Method: The method uses Event-Sourcing Agent Architecture (ESAA) to create a shared log (activity.jsonl) from visible conversations, which is then processed into task- and state-specific models without requiring LLM inference for capturing conversational flow.

Result: The approach enables heterogeneous agents to collaborate using shared logs, demonstrated through a 570 event case study. The public release includes implementation tools and preserves privacy by excluding private histories.

Conclusion: ESAA-Conversational facilitates interoperability and collaboration across diverse coding agents by leveraging shared, transparent, and privacy-preserving conversational logs.

Abstract: Software developers increasingly work with multiple LLM coding agents, switching among tools such as Codex, Grok, Claude Code, and other assistants as context windows fill, sessions end, or a particular agent becomes better suited to a subtask. Each agent, however, persists its conversation in a private and vendor-specific log. The result is conversational state drift: goals, decisions, open tasks, and rationales established with one agent are not reliably available when another agent takes over. This paper presents \emph{ESAA-Conversational}, a domain specialization of Event-Sourcing Agent Architecture (ESAA)~\cite{esaa} for shared conversational memory across heterogeneous agents. The method treats the visible conversation as a local event store: hooks and watchers capture visible turns, normalize them into an append-only \texttt{activity.jsonl}, and deterministically project read models such as \texttt{handoff.md}, \texttt{state.md}, \texttt{decisions.md}, and \texttt{tasks.json}. Mechanical capture does not require LLM inference; agents use judgment only for explicit curation, recording durable decisions and conversational tasks through domain commands. The public v1.1.0 release implements a PowerShell CLI with \texttt{init}, \texttt{enable-hooks}, \texttt{sync}, \texttt{project}, \texttt{verify}, \texttt{context}, \texttt{decide}, and \texttt{task}; includes \texttt{workspace\_root} isolation and a write-path lockfile; and is distributed as a greenfield package with an empty public log. A self-referential case study with 570 development-lab events shows that heterogeneous agents can collaborate through a shared log without a direct agent-to-agent channel, while the public distribution preserves privacy by excluding the private conversational history.

</details>


### [370] [From Task-Guided Conversational Graphs to Goal-Oriented Dialogue Runtimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23797)
*Mariano Garralda-Barrio*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper proposes GODR, a design pattern for managing complex goal-oriented dialogue in LLM workflows, supporting suspended, resumed, revised, and invalidated goals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing frameworks for large language model workflows struggle with maintaining conversational continuity in complex, goal-oriented scenarios involving interdependent objectives.

Method: The paper introduces GODR, a design pattern that organizes goals, task frames, lifecycle states, and resumption contracts as primary runtime objects, while delegating execution to graph runtimes, agents, tools, or APIs.

Result: The paper formalizes the problem, defines the GODR design pattern, and sets an agenda for empirical validation without providing performance claims.

Conclusion: GODR addresses conversational challenges in interruptible and multi-domain dialogues, complementing existing frameworks rather than replacing them for simple processes.

Abstract: Graph and multi-agent orchestration frameworks make production large language model (LLM) workflows practical, but they do not by themselves solve conversational continuity when users maintain several interdependent objectives. This conceptual systems paper focuses on the high-complexity end of that design space, where goals can be suspended, resumed, revised, and invalidated by actions in other goals. We introduce the Goal-Oriented Dialogue Runtime (GODR), a framework-neutral design pattern that treats goals, task frames, lifecycle state, invalidation rules, and resumption contracts as first-class runtime objects while delegating bounded execution to graph runtimes, agents, tools, or application programming interfaces (APIs). GODR is not proposed as a replacement for workflow graphs in simple guided processes; it is intended for complex, multi-domain, interruptible conversations where objective continuity cannot be recovered reliably from agent identity, chat history, or execution-graph position alone. The paper formalizes the problem, proposes runtime objects and architecture-selection criteria, and frames evaluation as an agenda for future empirical validation rather than as a measured performance claim.

</details>


### [371] [JupOtter: Cell-Level Bug Detection in Jupyter Notebooks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23877)
*Lukas Ottenhof,Thibaud Lutellier*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents JupOtter, a bug detection system for Jupyter Notebooks, featuring a specific tokenization strategy, a cell-level bug prediction method, and a labeled dataset of 21,000 notebooks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Jupyter Notebooks are increasingly used for complex programming tasks, leading to a rise in buggy notebook codes. Current bug detection tools are inadequate for the notebook environment.

Method: JupOtter includes a novel tokenization strategy that retains the structural integrity of notebook cells, a technique for predicting bugs at the cell level, and the new OtterDataset for training and evaluation.

Result: JupOtter achieves impressive cell-level bug detection performance, surpassing static analyzers and large language models on two out of three datasets.

Conclusion: JupOtter provides an effective bug detection solution tailored specifically for Jupyter Notebooks, addressing a growing need for improved debugging tools in this environment.

Abstract: Jupyter Notebooks are an increasingly popular coding environment used across many domains, especially in Python-based data science and scientific computing. Originally used for prototyping and interactive exploration, notebooks are increasingly used to develop more complex programs, leading to a rapid rise in buggy notebooks on platforms like GitHub. To address this trend, we present JupOtter, a bug detection system designed specifically for Jupyter Notebooks. JupOtter features three novel contributions: (1) a notebook-specific tokenization strategy that preserves cell structure, (2) a cell-level bug prediction technique, and (3) a new labeled dataset, OtterDataset, containing over 21,000 notebooks annotated for fine-grained cell-level bug detection. JupOtter achieves cell-level bug detection F1 scores that surpass static analyzers and large language models in two out of three evaluation datasets.

</details>


### [372] [Domain-Driven Design in Practice: A Mining Study of Maintenance and Evolution in Open-Source Repositories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23984)
*Weixing Zhang,Bowen Jiang,Yuhong Fu,Haowei Cheng,Mario Herb,Anne Koziolek*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper investigates how Domain-Driven Design (DDD) is applied in open-source GitHub repositories, focusing on its distribution, evolution, and impact on software maintenance using a large-scale empirical approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scarcity of large-scale empirical evidence on the practice and impact of DDD in software engineering hampers understanding and refinement of the methodology, particularly for open-source projects.

Method: The study involves collecting DDD-related GitHub repositories through automated filtering and manual assessment and analyzing them with four research questions addressing DDD building block usage, evolution, boundary violations, and maintenance correlations.

Result: The research identifies patterns in how DDD is utilized, its changes over time, the prevalence of boundary violations, and their impact on maintenance quality in open-source projects.

Conclusion: The findings will guide future improvements in DDD methodology and tool development, bridging existing gaps in large-scale empirical knowledge on DDD.

Abstract: Domain-Driven Design (DDD) is an influential software development methodology that structures software around business domain complexity through tactical building blocks such as Entities, Value Objects, Aggregates, and Repositories. Despite its prominence in software engineering, large-scale empirical evidence on how DDD is practiced, how it evolves, and how it relates to software maintenance quality in open-source projects remains scarce. This study presents a pre-registered empirical investigation of the distribution, evolution, and maintenance implications of DDD tactical building blocks in open-source GitHub repositories, addressing the call for large-scale empirical evaluations identified in a recent systematic literature review. We will collect DDD-related repositories from GitHub using the Search API, apply automated keyword filtering and manual relevance assessment, and analyze the resulting dataset through four research questions covering: (RQ1) the static distribution and co-usage of DDD building blocks across repository types, (RQ2) their longitudinal evolution over commit history, (RQ3) the extent and maintenance implications of Bounded Context boundary violations (the primary technical challenge in DDD adoption, unquantified at scale), and (RQ4) the temporal association between maintenance activities and building block churn or Bounded Context violations in DDD repositories. Together, these RQs trace the full maintenance and evolution lifecycle of open-source DDD projects and establish an empirical foundation for future DDD tool support and methodology refinement.

</details>


### [373] [Collaborative and AI-Supported Requirements Elicitation: An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24060)
*Manoel Salgado Neto,Alan Araujo,Ronnie de Souza Santos*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This study investigates the impact of AI-supported collaborative environments on requirements elicitation by comparing four approaches, finding that AI-enhanced collaboration yields superior requirements outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore how advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) can be leveraged to improve the outcomes of requirements elicitation processes, enhancing both collaboration and artifact quality.

Method: A mixed-method controlled experiment was conducted comparing four elicitation approaches: traditional collaboration, AI-supported collaboration using a GPT-powered tool, direct requirements generation with an LLM, and LLM-based artifact generation from collaborative transcripts. Results were evaluated using ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 quality criteria and participant feedback.

Result: AI-assisted collaborative approaches produced the highest-rated requirements artifacts, which were also perceived as clearer and easier to implement compared to traditional methods.

Conclusion: Generative AI can enhance the transformation of collaborative knowledge into structured requirements, demonstrating the potential to improve Requirements Engineering through AI-supported collaboration.

Abstract: Requirements elicitation requires stakeholders to communicate needs, negotiate priorities, and collaboratively construct knowledge that can be transformed into software requirements artifacts. Recent advances in LLMs have created opportunities to support these activities through AI-assisted collaboration and automated artifact generation. However, limited empirical evidence is available regarding how AI-supported collaborative environments influence requirements elicitation outcomes. In this study, we conducted a mixed-method controlled experiment comparing four requirements elicitation approaches: collaborative elicitation without AI support, collaborative elicitation supported by the Strateegia platform and its GPT-powered Writer applet, direct requirements generation using a Large Language Model, and requirements generation from collaborative discussion transcripts using a Large Language Model. We evaluated the resulting artifacts using quality criteria derived from ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 and collected participant perceptions regarding the elicitation process. Our findings indicate that approaches combining stakeholder collaboration and AI-supported synthesis produced the highest-rated requirements artifacts and were perceived as clearer and easier to execute than traditional collaborative elicitation. The results suggest that generative AI can support the transformation of collaboratively generated knowledge into structured requirements documentation while preserving the value of stakeholder participation. We discuss implications for AI-supported requirements elicitation and human-AI collaboration in Requirements Engineering.

</details>


### [374] [Agon: An Autonomous Large-Scale Omnidisciplinary Research System Built on Prompt Economy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24177)
*Youran Sun,Xingyu Ren,Chugang Yi,Jiaxuan Guo,Kejia Zhang,Jianda Du,Haizhao Yang*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper presents Agon, a research orchestrator aimed at enhancing scalability by validating claims and leaving complex judgments to human scientists.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the bottleneck of assessing research claims instead of producing research artifacts in an era of large language models.

Method: The authors built Agon on six design principles, and tested it with 444 iterations across domains using small starting topics and no human-written experimental code.

Result: The study demonstrated scalability, identified new failure classes, and created a taxonomy to categorize these failures.

Conclusion: Agon represents a step toward a paradigm where machines handle scalability tasks, leaving humans to steer and make nuanced judgments.

Abstract: Large language models are making research production scalable, shifting the bottleneck from producing artifacts to judging claims. We present \textsc{Agon}, a research orchestrator that validates what can be checked inside the workflow and leaves the remaining judgments to human scientists. \textsc{Agon} is built on six design principles: Prompt Economy, Future-Facing, Minimal Prompts, OmniDisciplinary, Massive Parallelism, and Zero-Code. We ran \textsc{Agon} across domains for 444 iterations of Prompt Economy loops, using only small starting topics and no human-written experimental code. These deployments demonstrate scalability while exposing new classes of failure. We organize these failures into a taxonomy along severity, fixability, visibility, and capability locus. The taxonomy separates failures the loops can see and fix from those that require human judgment. Together, these results show that \textsc{Agon} is pushing research toward a new paradigm: machine scales, human steers.

</details>


### [375] [AutoSpec: Safety Rule Evolution for LLM Agents via Inductive Logic Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24245)
*Pingchuan Ma,Zhaoyu Wang,Zimo Ji,Yuguang Zhou,Zhantong Xue,Zongjie Li,Shuai Wang,Xiaoqin Zhang*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents a framework, AutoSpec, to evolve LLM agent safety rules automatically, achieving higher F1 scores and interpretable results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the safety risks posed by autonomous LLM agents, such as executing harmful actions or violating constraints. Current manual or neural-based safety approaches have significant tradeoffs in interpretability and reliability.

Method: AutoSpec uses counterexample-guided inductive synthesis (CEGIS) and inductive logic programming (ILP) to iteratively refine safety rules based on user annotations, mining counterexamples to optimize precision and recall.

Result: AutoSpec improved rule F1 scores to 0.98 and 0.93 in experiments across two domains, significantly reducing false positives (up to 94%) while maintaining high recall, converging in 4-5 iterations.

Conclusion: AutoSpec offers a robust, interpretable, and effective approach to refining safety rules for LLM agents, outperforming existing methods and providing generalizable, human-understandable rules.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly automate complex tasks by integrating language models with external tools and environments. However, their autonomy poses significant safety risks: agents may execute destructive commands, leak sensitive data, or violate domain constraints. Existing safety approaches face a fundamental tradeoff: hand-crafted rules are interpretable but brittle, with overly conservative rules blocking safe operations (high false positives) while permissive rules miss unsafe behaviors (high false negatives). Neural classifiers lack the interpretability required for safety-critical deployments.
  We present AutoSpec, a framework that automatically evolves deployed expert-designed safety rules from user safe/unsafe annotations through counterexample-guided inductive synthesis (CEGIS) guided by inductive logic programming (ILP). Starting from the expert rules and a stream of annotated traces, AutoSpec iteratively evaluates rules, mines false-positive and false-negative counterexamples, uses ILP to learn which predicates discriminate them, generates candidate rule edits, and verifies candidates to select the best revision. The key insight is that ILP efficiently identifies predicates that appear frequently in false negatives but rarely in false positives (or vice versa), dramatically pruning the exponential search space of rule edits. This continues until convergence, producing interpretable rules that balance precision and recall.
  We evaluate AutoSpec on 291 execution traces spanning code execution and embodied agent domains. AutoSpec raises rule F1 to 0.98 and 0.93 across the two domains, achieving up to 94% false positive reduction while maintaining high recall, and converges within 4-5 iterations. The ILP-guided approach achieves up to 4.8x higher F1 than heuristic CEGIS. The learned rules are human-readable, auditable, and generalize to unseen scenarios.

</details>


### [376] [Architecting Hybrid Quantum-Classical Software Systems: Exploration of the Design Trade-off Space with Quantitative Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24260)
*Álvaro M. Aparicio-Morales,Jose Garcia-Alonso,Javier Cámara,Juan M. Murillo*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper presents a method for designing hybrid quantum-classical applications using an architectural style approach, enabling dynamic and QoS-based system configuration selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations in functionality and hardware constraints of quantum computing by integrating it with classical computing through a hybrid software system.

Method: The paper formalizes an architectural style for hybrid quantum-classical applications to analyze design space and support trade-off decisions for system configurations based on SOA principles.

Result: The method identifies decision boundaries effectively and allows for the dynamic selection of optimal configurations according to quality-of-service (QoS) criteria.

Conclusion: The proposed method facilitates hybrid system design by overcoming constraints of quantum hardware and leveraging architectural decision-making to achieve better application performance.

Abstract: Addressing problems beyond classical computing limits is sparking an increasing interest in Quantum Computing. However, despite their adequacy to address specific problems, quantum algorithms cover a limited subset of the functionality required in real-world computing systems. Additionally, they require expensive specialized hardware. To overcome this issue, hybrid (quantum-classical) software systems are emerging as a promising way to integrate both computing paradigms by applying the principles of Service-Oriented Architectures (SOA). Still, the design and deployment of hybrid service-based systems faces unique challenges like the idiosyncrasies and constraints of NISQ computers (e.g., algorithms that can only run in specific machines, disparate quality attribute metrics), and the management of structural and behavioural properties of service-based applications. From the SOA perspective, architectural decisions need to be made by performing a trade-off analysis and providing quantitative guarantees of system configurations under prescribed levels of uncertainty. In this paper, a method to explore the design space of quantum-classical applications is provided by a formalization of an architectural style of hybrid applications. The obtained results demonstrate that the proposed method successfully identifies decision boundaries. It enables the dynamic selection of the most suitable hybrid or classical configuration based on the user's QoS criteria.

</details>


### [377] [Detecting AI Coding Agents in Open Source: A Validated Multi-Method Census of 180 Million Repositories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24429)
*Arsham Khosravani,Audris Mockus*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a multi-layered detection framework for identifying generative AI coding agents in the open-source supply chain and finds significant discrepancies between detection methods, showing the dominance of certain agents like Claude Code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of understanding around the prevalence and behavior of generative AI coding agents in the open-source ecosystem and mitigate limitations of current single-signal detection methods.

Method: The authors developed a comprehensive detection framework combining configuration-file scanning, commit-message analysis, author-identity matching, and bot-signature lookup. They performed hand-validation on the results to ensure precision.

Result: The framework identified over 320,000 agent-generated commits monthly, with Claude Code dominating in both total commit counts and silent adoption. Single-signal detection methods were shown to drastically undercount activity, often by over 30x.

Conclusion: Generative AI agents are prevalent but are captured through diverse and non-overlapping channels. Single-method approaches significantly underrepresent their impact, emphasizing the need for multi-method detection to accurately assess activity and contributions.

Abstract: Generative AI coding agents are entering the open-source supply chain, yet their diverse and often invisible traces leave their prevalence poorly understood. We introduce a multi-layered detection framework that integrates configuration-file scanning, commit-message analysis, author-identity matching, and bot-signature lookup across World of Code (180M+ Git repositories), classifying agent traces into four behavioral types. No single method captures more than a fraction of activity: multi-method detection identifies 850,157 Claude Code commits in one snapshot, of which bot-account lookup_the signal most adoption studies rely on_recovers only 28,154 (3.3%), a 30x relative-recall gap, so single-signal prevalence estimates are biased low by at least this factor. Every detection pattern is hand-validated (495 labels) with per-cell precision and Wilson confidence intervals. Across snapshots from December 2024 to April 2026, commit-attributed agents generate over 320,000 commits per month; Claude Code leads (886,122 commits across 17,295 projects) and dominates silent, configuration-file-only adoption (21,078 projects). Compared against an independent pull-request census (AIDev), the two channels capture nearly disjoint agent populations_a PR census misses 79% of commit-detected Claude Code adopters and essentially all Codex adopters_and different kinds of work: PR-deployed cloud agents (Codex, Cursor) surface as feature work, while commit-deployed in-editor agents (Claude Code, OpenHands, Aider) surface as maintenance. The observed work profile follows deployment and detection mode rather than the tool itself, so no single channel is representative.

</details>


### [378] [Agentic Generation of AST Transformation Rules for Fixing Breaking Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24446)
*Frank Reyes,Benoit Baudry,Martin Monperrus*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: BigBag is a framework that generates reusable fixing transformations for breaking API updates in software libraries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Software projects often face issues due to breaking API changes in third-party library updates, requiring scalable patching methods.

Method: BigBag creates agentic fixing transformations using large language models and AST transformation engines to encode repair logic.
