TL;DR: The paper discusses how artificial intelligence (AI) can enhance augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) systems and proposes evaluation methods that consider users' intersectional and nuanced desires.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of evaluating AI-powered AAC interfaces, recognizing the intersectional and multifaceted needs of users.

Method: The authors examine six AAC problem spaces, propose the use of AI in these contexts, and suggest evaluation methods accounting for user intersectionality.

Result: Contributions include robust evaluation approaches for AI-based AAC systems that capture nuanced user desires and address broader issues within the problem spaces.

Conclusion: AI technology can improve AAC systems if the complexity of user needs is respected. Proposed evaluation techniques can lead to better user-centered design outcomes.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) can enhance what people who use augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) are able to do with their systems. However, evaluating AI-powered AAC interfaces can be difficult. People are intersectional beings and current evaluation metrics can struggle to capture the multifaceted and nuanced desires people may have for their AAC. We explore the complicated nature of six AAC problem spaces, explore how AI might be used in these spaces, and suggest more robust methods of evaluation that take the intersectional nuances of people into account. We also discuss broader issues that arise across these problem spaces and how they could be addressed using our proposed evaluation methods.

</details>


<div id='cs.NI'></div>

# cs.NI [[Back]](#toc)

### [469] [SkyChain Intelligence: A Blockchain-Secured Multi-Agent DRL Framework for Low-Altitude Embodied Artificial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24193)
*Haoxiang Luo,Tianqi Jiang,Ruichen Zhang,Yinqiu Liu,Gang Sun,Hongfang Yu,Abbas Jamalipour,Dong In Kim*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper introduces SkyChain Intelligence, a holistic framework integrating AI, blockchain, and multi-agent reinforcement learning to improve the autonomy, security, and efficiency of resource-constrained agents in low-altitude computational networks (LACNets).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation arises from the need to address the trilemma of autonomy, security, and efficiency faced by resource-constrained Low-Altitude Embodied AI agents in decentralized networks, where existing solutions often fail to optimize trust, performance, and overhead simultaneously.

Method: The authors propose a decentralized framework combining agentic AI, consortium blockchain, and MADRL. A blockchain-based trust management system with a reputation mechanism is designed, and a hybrid-action-space MADDPG algorithm is implemented, embedding reputation into decision-making for optimized offloading, resource allocation, and navigation.

Result: The framework outperforms existing baselines in metrics like task completion latency and energy consumption. It achieves a 94.1% task completion rate and shows stable convergence within 300 training episodes.

Conclusion: SkyChain Intelligence offers a promising solution for creating secure, autonomous, and efficient machine-to-machine ecosystems in the low-altitude domain, addressing critical trade-offs in decentralized environments with malicious agents.

Abstract: With the rapid development of the Low-Altitude Economy (LAE) ecosystem, Low-Altitude Embodied Artificial Intelligence (LAEAI) agents have become the core carriers of autonomous aerial services, thereby enabling dynamic Low-altitude Computility Networks (LACNets) for distributed computing resource sharing. However, resource-constrained LAEAI agents in decentralized LACNets face a fundamental trilemma of autonomy, security, and efficiency. Existing solutions primarily focus on either optimizing computational performance or enhancing security in isolation, failing to address the inherent trade-offs among trust, performance, and overhead in untrusted dynamic environments with malicious agents. To tackle this challenge, this paper proposes SkyChain Intelligence, a holistic framework that synergistically integrates agentic AI, consortium blockchain, and Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (MADRL). We design a lightweight blockchain-based decentralized trust management system with a dynamic reputation mechanism and develop a hybrid-action-space MADDPG algorithm that embeds on-chain reputation scores into the reward function to jointly optimize offloading decisions, resource allocation, and drone 3D trajectories. Extensive simulations demonstrate that our framework outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in task completion latency and energy consumption, while achieving a 94.1% task completion rate in the baseline scenario and stable convergence within 300 training episodes. This work provides a viable path for building secure, autonomous, and efficient machine-to-machine computing ecosystems in the low-altitude domain.

</details>


### [470] [Importance of Intent-Sharing for V2X-based Maneuver Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24203)
*Rafael Molina-Masegosa,Sergei S. Avedisov,Miguel Sepulcre,Javier Gozalvez,Yashar Z. Farid,Onur Altintas*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper highlights the benefits of intent-sharing among connected and automated vehicles (CAVs) in improving maneuver coordination compared to trajectory prediction methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of maneuver coordination among CAVs, which requires accurate knowledge of other vehicles' driving intentions.

Method: The study compares the effectiveness of intent-sharing in coordinated lane changes with a trajectory prediction method using kinematic data in a highway scenario.

Result: Substantial improvements in maneuver coordination were observed when intent-sharing was used, as quantified by the percentage of successful lane changes in two scenarios.

Conclusion: Intent-sharing is crucial and should be integrated into maneuver coordination protocols for CAVs to achieve more effective and reliable operations.

Abstract: This paper examines the critical role of intent-sharing in enabling effective maneuver coordination for connected and automated vehicles (CAVs). Successful maneuver coordinations require vehicles to accurately know other vehicles' driving intentions. Intent-sharing can be achieved by the remote vehicles directly communicating their plans with the ego vehicle, as opposed to the ego vehicle predicting the trajectory on the remote vehicles' behalf. In this paper, we investigate the potential of intent-sharing on maneuver coordination effectiveness by quantifying the percentage of successful coordinations. We analyze the potential of intent-sharing by comparing its effectiveness for coordinated lane changes in a highway scenario with the effectiveness of a trajectory prediction method based on current kinematic data. Our analysis demonstrates in two scenarios substantial improvements in maneuver coordination when CAVs have direct access to the nearby vehicles' driving intentions through intent sharing. These findings highlight the importance of including intent-sharing in the maneuver coordination protocol.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [471] [Flow-Corrected Thompson Sampling for Non-Stationary Contextual Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23933)
*AmirHossein Naghdi,Ali Baheri*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper introduces Flow-Corrected Thompson Sampling (fcTS), a Bayesian method designed for non-stationary linear contextual bandits, improving performance by correcting and reweighting historical observations instead of discarding them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing contextual bandit algorithms struggle with systematic bias when reward models evolve or drift over time, requiring a better approach to utilize historical data in the presence of non-stationarity.

Method: The authors propose fcTS, which reuses historical data by modeling reward drift over time using confidence-weighted transport mechanisms and integrating aspects like slope estimation, phase-aware reuse, and changepoint detection into posterior updates.

Result: fcTS demonstrated superior performance across five case studies and a portfolio-selection benchmark, particularly excelling in scenarios with recurring temporal structures when compared to traditional forgetting-based approaches.

Conclusion: Structured correction and reweighting of historical observations can lead to significantly more sample-efficient learning in non-stationary environments compared to simply discarding old data.

Abstract: We study non-stationary linear contextual bandits where the reward model drifts over time, rendering classical contextual bandit algorithms brittle because historical data becomes systematically biased. We propose Flow-Corrected Thompson Sampling (fcTS), a Bayesian method that reuses experience by transporting past rewards to the present using an explicit drift model and incorporating each transported observation with a confidence weight that reflects transport reliability. This yields a unified template that specializes in (i) linear parameter drift via online slope estimation and reward correction, (ii) periodic variation via phase-aware reuse across cycles, and (iii) recurring regime switches via changepoint detection and regime-specific posterior memory. The resulting posterior updates remain closed-form under a linear Gaussian model and can be implemented efficiently with truncated, incrementally updated sufficient statistics. Across five controlled case studies and a semi-synthetic portfolio-selection benchmark with multiple overlapping non-stationarities, fcTS outperforms standard forgetting-based baselines (discounting, sliding windows, and periodic restarts), with the largest gains in settings exhibiting recurring temporal structure. These results demonstrate that when non-stationarity is structured, correcting and reweighting historical observations can be substantially more sample-efficient than uniformly discarding them.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.BM'></div>

# q-bio.BM [[Back]](#toc)

### [472] [JEDEL: Zero-Shot DNA-Encoded Library Design for Early-Stage Drug Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23745)
*Zygimantas Jocys,Zhanxing Zhu,Henriette M. G. Willems,Katayoun Farrahi*

Main category: q-bio.BM

TL;DR: The paper introduces JEDEL, a framework that converts 3D pharmacophores into synthesis-ready DNA-encoded libraries (DELs) using purchasable building blocks and validated chemical reactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable the generation of synthesis-ready DNA-encoded libraries directly usable in experiments by aligning pharmacophore geometry with molecular structure and providing combinatorial synthesis instructions.

Method: The researchers developed JEDEL, which maps pharmacophore interaction patterns to molecular synthesis routes using validated chemical reactions and purchasable building blocks. The framework decodes this into actionable synthesis instructions and generates libraries without the need for target-specific retraining.

Result: JEDEL produced targeted libraries for 18 protein targets, outperforming random and diversity-based approaches in metrics like binding affinity, pharmacophore recovery, and sample efficiency, while ensuring experimental realizability.

Conclusion: JEDEL shifts the paradigm from virtual molecule production to designing experimentally deployable libraries, effectively addressing challenges in generating drug-targeted molecular libraries at scale.

Abstract: We present JEDEL, a framework for generating synthesis-ready DNA-encoded libraries (DELs) directly from three-dimensional pharmacophore representations of active ligands. JEDEL is the first model to map pharmacophore interaction patterns to actionable, scalable synthesis instructions, enabling the design of targeted libraries comprising potentially millions of molecules. Unlike existing generative approaches that produce virtual compounds requiring downstream synthesis planning, JEDEL operates within the space of purchasable building blocks and validated reactions, ensuring that every output is experimentally realizable by construction. JEDEL learns a predictive alignment between pharmacophore geometry and molecular structure and decodes this into combinatorial synthesis routes at scale. Across 18 protein targets, it generates focused libraries that outperform random and diversity-based baselines in predicted binding affinity, pharmacophore recovery, and sample efficiency, without target-specific retraining. JEDEL enables a shift from virtual molecule generation to experimentally deployable library design.

</details>
